1.play: BEDFORD: Hung be the heavens with black, yield day to night!

2.play: Comets, importing change of times and states,

3.play: Brandish your crystal tresses in the sky,

4.play: And with them scourge the bad revolting stars

5.play: That have consented unto Henry's death!

6.play: King Henry the Fifth, too famous to live long!

7.play: England ne'er lost a king of so much worth.

8.play: GLOUCESTER: England ne'er had a king until his time.

9.play: Virtue he had, deserving to command:

10.play: His brandish'd sword did blind men with his beams:

11.play: His arms spread wider than a dragon's wings;

12.play: His sparking eyes, replete with wrathful fire,

13.play: More dazzled and drove back his enemies

14.play: Than mid-day sun fierce bent against their faces.

15.play: What should I say? his deeds exceed all speech:

16.play: He ne'er lift up his hand but conquered.

17.play: EXETER: We mourn in black: why mourn we not in blood?

18.play: Henry is dead and never shall revive:

19.play: Upon a wooden coffin we attend,

20.play: And death's dishonourable victory

21.play: We with our stately presence glorify,

22.play: Like captives bound to a triumphant car.

23.play: What! shall we curse the planets of mishap

24.play: That plotted thus our glory's overthrow?

25.play: Or shall we think the subtle-witted French

26.play: Conjurers and sorcerers, that afraid of him

27.play: By magic verses have contrived his end?

28.play: BISHOP

29.play: OF WINCHESTER: He was a king bless'd of the King of kings.

30.play: Unto the French the dreadful judgement-day

31.play: So dreadful will not be as was his sight.

32.play: The battles of the Lord of hosts he fought:

33.play: The church's prayers made him so prosperous.

34.play: GLOUCESTER: The church! where is it? Had not churchmen pray'd,

35.play: His thread of life had not so soon decay'd:

36.play: None do you like but an effeminate prince,

37.play: Whom, like a school-boy, you may over-awe.

38.play: BISHOP

39.play: OF WINCHESTER: Gloucester, whate'er we like, thou art protector

40.play: And lookest to command the prince and realm.

41.play: Thy wife is proud; she holdeth thee in awe,

42.play: More than God or religious churchmen may.

43.play: GLOUCESTER: Name not religion, for thou lovest the flesh,

44.play: And ne'er throughout the year to church thou go'st

45.play: Except it be to pray against thy foes.

46.play: BEDFORD: Cease, cease these jars and rest your minds in peace:

47.play: Let's to the altar: heralds, wait on us:

48.play: Instead of gold, we'll offer up our arms:

49.play: Since arms avail not now that Henry's dead.

50.play: Posterity, await for wretched years,

51.play: When at their mothers' moist eyes babes shall suck,

52.play: Our isle be made a nourish of salt tears,

53.play: And none but women left to wail the dead.

54.play: Henry the Fifth, thy ghost I invocate:

55.play: Prosper this realm, keep it from civil broils,

56.play: Combat with adverse planets in the heavens!

57.play: A far more glorious star thy soul will make

59.play: What say'st thou, man, before dead Henry's corse?

60.play: Speak softly, or the loss of those great towns

61.play: Will make him burst his lead and rise from death.

62.play: GLOUCESTER: Is Paris lost? is Rouen yielded up?

63.play: If Henry were recall'd to life again,

64.play: These news would cause him once more yield the ghost.

65.play: EXETER: How were they lost? what treachery was used?

66.play: Were our tears wanting to this funeral,

67.play: These tidings would call forth their flowing tides.

68.play: BEDFORD: Me they concern; Regent I am of France.

69.play: Give me my steeled coat. I'll fight for France.

70.play: Away with these disgraceful wailing robes!

71.play: Wounds will I lend the French instead of eyes,

73.play: EXETER: The Dauphin crowned king! all fly to him!

74.play: O, whither shall we fly from this reproach?

75.play: GLOUCESTER: We will not fly, but to our enemies' throats.

76.play: Bedford, if thou be slack, I'll fight it out.

77.play: BEDFORD: Gloucester, why doubt'st thou of my forwardness?

78.play: An army have I muster'd in my thoughts,

80.play: OF WINCHESTER: What! wherein Talbot overcame? is't so?

81.play: BEDFORD: Is Talbot slain? then I will slay myself,

82.play: For living idly here in pomp and ease,

83.play: Whilst such a worthy leader, wanting aid,

84.play: Unto his dastard foemen is betray'd.

85.play: His ransom there is none but I shall pay:

86.play: I'll hale the Dauphin headlong from his throne:

87.play: His crown shall be the ransom of my friend;

88.play: Four of their lords I'll change for one of ours.

89.play: Farewell, my masters; to my task will I;

90.play: Bonfires in France forthwith I am to make,

91.play: To keep our great Saint George's feast withal:

92.play: Ten thousand soldiers with me I will take,

93.play: Whose bloody deeds shall make all Europe quake.

94.play: EXETER: Remember, lords, your oaths to Henry sworn,

95.play: Either to quell the Dauphin utterly,

96.play: Or bring him in obedience to your yoke.

97.play: BEDFORD: I do remember it; and here take my leave,

99.play: GLOUCESTER: I'll to the Tower with all the haste I can,

100.play: To view the artillery and munition;

101.play: And then I will proclaim young Henry king.

102.play: EXETER: To Eltham will I, where the young king is,

104.play: And for his safety there I'll best devise.

106.play: OF WINCHESTER: Each hath his place and function to attend:

107.play: I am left out; for me nothing remains.

108.play: But long I will not be Jack out of office:

109.play: The king from Eltham I intend to steal

110.play: And sit at chiefest stern of public weal.

1.play: CHARLES: Mars his true moving, even as in the heavens

2.play: So in the earth, to this day is not known:

3.play: Late did he shine upon the English side;

4.play: Now we are victors; upon us he smiles.

5.play: What towns of any moment but we have?

6.play: At pleasure here we lie near Orleans;

7.play: Otherwhiles the famish'd English, like pale ghosts,

8.play: Faintly besiege us one hour in a month.

9.play: ALENCON: They want their porridge and their fat bull-beeves:

10.play: Either they must be dieted like mules

11.play: And have their provender tied to their mouths

12.play: Or piteous they will look, like drowned mice.

13.play: REIGNIER: Let's raise the siege: why live we idly here?

14.play: Talbot is taken, whom we wont to fear:

15.play: Remaineth none but mad-brain'd Salisbury;

16.play: And he may well in fretting spend his gall,

17.play: Nor men nor money hath he to make war.

18.play: CHARLES: Sound, sound alarum! we will rush on them.

19.play: Now for the honour of the forlorn French!

20.play: Him I forgive my death that killeth me

21.play: When he sees me go back one foot or fly.

22.play: Who ever saw the like? what men have I!

23.play: Dogs! cowards! dastards! I would ne'er have fled,

24.play: But that they left me 'midst my enemies.

25.play: REIGNIER: Salisbury is a desperate homicide;

26.play: He fighteth as one weary of his life.

27.play: The other lords, like lions wanting food,

28.play: Do rush upon us as their hungry prey.

29.play: ALENCON: Froissart, a countryman of ours, records,

30.play: England all Olivers and Rowlands bred,

31.play: During the time Edward the Third did reign.

32.play: More truly now may this be verified;

33.play: For none but Samsons and Goliases

34.play: It sendeth forth to skirmish. One to ten!

35.play: Lean, raw-boned rascals! who would e'er suppose

36.play: They had such courage and audacity?

37.play: CHARLES: Let's leave this town; for they are hare-brain'd slaves,

38.play: And hunger will enforce them to be more eager:

39.play: Of old I know them; rather with their teeth

40.play: The walls they'll tear down than forsake the siege.

41.play: REIGNIER: I think, by some odd gimmors or device

42.play: Their arms are set like clocks, stiff to strike on;

43.play: Else ne'er could they hold out so as they do.

44.play: By my consent, we'll even let them alone.

45.play: ALENCON: Be it so.

46.play: BASTARD OF ORLEANS: Where's the Prince Dauphin? I have news for him.

47.play: CHARLES: Bastard of Orleans, thrice welcome to us.

48.play: BASTARD OF ORLEANS: Methinks your looks are sad, your cheer appall'd:

49.play: Hath the late overthrow wrought this offence?

50.play: Be not dismay'd, for succor is at hand:

51.play: A holy maid hither with me I bring,

52.play: Which by a vision sent to her from heaven

53.play: Ordained is to raise this tedious siege

54.play: And drive the English forth the bounds of France.

55.play: The spirit of deep prophecy she hath,

56.play: Exceeding the nine sibyls of old Rome:

57.play: What's past and what's to come she can descry.

58.play: Speak, shall I call her in? Believe my words,

59.play: For they are certain and unfallible.

61.play: But first, to try her skill,

62.play: Reignier, stand thou as Dauphin in my place:

63.play: Question her proudly; let thy looks be stern:

64.play: By this means shall we sound what skill she hath.

65.play: REIGNIER: Fair maid, is't thou wilt do these wondrous feats?

66.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: Reignier, is't thou that thinkest to beguile me?

67.play: Where is the Dauphin? Come, come from behind;

68.play: I know thee well, though never seen before.

69.play: Be not amazed, there's nothing hid from me:

70.play: In private will I talk with thee apart.

71.play: Stand back, you lords, and give us leave awhile.

72.play: REIGNIER: She takes upon her bravely at first dash.

73.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: Dauphin, I am by birth a shepherd's daughter,

74.play: My wit untrain'd in any kind of art.

75.play: Heaven and our Lady gracious hath it pleased

76.play: To shine on my contemptible estate:

77.play: Lo, whilst I waited on my tender lambs,

78.play: And to sun's parching heat display'd my cheeks,

79.play: God's mother deigned to appear to me

80.play: And in a vision full of majesty

81.play: Will'd me to leave my base vocation

82.play: And free my country from calamity:

83.play: Her aid she promised and assured success:

84.play: In complete glory she reveal'd herself;

85.play: And, whereas I was black and swart before,

86.play: With those clear rays which she infused on me

87.play: That beauty am I bless'd with which you see.

88.play: Ask me what question thou canst possible,

90.play: My courage try by combat, if thou darest,

91.play: And thou shalt find that I exceed my sex.

92.play: Resolve on this, thou shalt be fortunate,

93.play: If thou receive me for thy warlike mate.

94.play: CHARLES: Thou hast astonish'd me with thy high terms:

95.play: Only this proof I'll of thy valour make,

96.play: In single combat thou shalt buckle with me,

97.play: And if thou vanquishest, thy words are true;

99.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: I am prepared: here is my keen-edged sword,

100.play: Deck'd with five flower-de-luces on each side;

101.play: The which at Touraine, in Saint Katharine's

103.play: Out of a great deal of old iron I chose forth.

104.play: CHARLES: Then come, o' God's name; I fear no woman.

105.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: And while I live, I'll ne'er fly from a man.

106.play: CHARLES: Stay, stay thy hands! thou art an Amazon

107.play: And fightest with the sword of Deborah.

108.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: Christ's mother helps me, else I were too weak.

109.play: CHARLES: Whoe'er helps thee, 'tis thou that must help me:

110.play: Impatiently I burn with thy desire;

111.play: My heart and hands thou hast at once subdued.

112.play: Excellent Pucelle, if thy name be so,

113.play: Let me thy servant and not sovereign be:

114.play: 'Tis the French Dauphin sueth to thee thus.

115.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: I must not yield to any rites of love,

116.play: For my profession's sacred from above:

117.play: When I have chased all thy foes from hence,

118.play: Then will I think upon a recompense.

119.play: CHARLES: Meantime look gracious on thy prostrate thrall.

120.play: REIGNIER: My lord, methinks, is very long in talk.

121.play: ALENCON: Doubtless he shrives this woman to her smock;

122.play: Else ne'er could he so long protract his speech.

123.play: REIGNIER: Shall we disturb him, since he keeps no mean?

124.play: ALENCON: He may mean more than we poor men do know:

125.play: These women are shrewd tempters with their tongues.

126.play: REIGNIER: My lord, where are you? what devise you on?

127.play: Shall we give over Orleans, or no?

128.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: Why, no, I say, distrustful recreants!

129.play: Fight till the last gasp; I will be your guard.

130.play: CHARLES: What she says I'll confirm: we'll fight it out.

131.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: Assign'd am I to be the English scourge.

132.play: This night the siege assuredly I'll raise:

133.play: Expect Saint Martin's summer, halcyon days,

134.play: Since I have entered into these wars.

135.play: Glory is like a circle in the water,

136.play: Which never ceaseth to enlarge itself

137.play: Till by broad spreading it disperse to nought.

138.play: With Henry's death the English circle ends;

139.play: Dispersed are the glories it included.

140.play: Now am I like that proud insulting ship

141.play: Which Caesar and his fortune bare at once.

142.play: CHARLES: Was Mahomet inspired with a dove?

143.play: Thou with an eagle art inspired then.

144.play: Helen, the mother of great Constantine,

145.play: Nor yet Saint Philip's daughters, were like thee.

146.play: Bright star of Venus, fall'n down on the earth,

147.play: How may I reverently worship thee enough?

148.play: ALENCON: Leave off delays, and let us raise the siege.

149.play: REIGNIER: Woman, do what thou canst to save our honours;

150.play: Drive them from Orleans and be immortalized.

151.play: CHARLES: Presently we'll try: come, let's away about it:

152.play: No prophet will I trust, if she prove false.

1.play: [Enter GLOUCESTER, with his Serving-men in blue coats]

2.play: GLOUCESTER: I am come to survey the Tower this day:

3.play: Since Henry's death, I fear, there is conveyance.

4.play: Where be these warders, that they wait not here?

5.play: Open the gates; 'tis Gloucester that calls.

6.play: Who willed you? or whose will stands but mine?

7.play: There's none protector of the realm but I.

8.play: Break up the gates, I'll be your warrantize.

9.play: Shall I be flouted thus by dunghill grooms?

10.play: WOODVILE: What noise is this? what traitors have we here?

11.play: GLOUCESTER: Lieutenant, is it you whose voice I hear?

12.play: Open the gates; here's Gloucester that would enter.

13.play: WOODVILE: Have patience, noble duke; I may not open;

14.play: The Cardinal of Winchester forbids:

15.play: From him I have express commandment

16.play: That thou nor none of thine shall be let in.

17.play: GLOUCESTER: Faint-hearted Woodvile, prizest him 'fore me?

18.play: Arrogant Winchester, that haughty prelate,

19.play: Whom Henry, our late sovereign, ne'er could brook?

20.play: Thou art no friend to God or to the king:

21.play: Open the gates, or I'll shut thee out shortly.

22.play: Serving-Men	Open the gates unto the lord protector,

23.play: Or we'll burst them open, if that you come not quickly.

24.play: BISHOP

25.play: OF WINCHESTER: How now, ambitious Humphry! what means this?

26.play: GLOUCESTER: Peel'd priest, dost thou command me to be shut out?

27.play: BISHOP

28.play: OF WINCHESTER: I do, thou most usurping proditor,

29.play: And not protector, of the king or realm.

30.play: GLOUCESTER: Stand back, thou manifest conspirator,

31.play: Thou that contrivedst to murder our dead lord;

32.play: Thou that givest whores indulgences to sin:

33.play: I'll canvass thee in thy broad cardinal's hat,

34.play: If thou proceed in this thy insolence.

35.play: BISHOP

36.play: OF WINCHESTER: Nay, stand thou back, I will not budge a foot:

37.play: This be Damascus, be thou cursed Cain,

38.play: To slay thy brother Abel, if thou wilt.

39.play: GLOUCESTER: I will not slay thee, but I'll drive thee back:

40.play: Thy scarlet robes as a child's bearing-cloth

41.play: I'll use to carry thee out of this place.

42.play: BISHOP

43.play: OF WINCHESTER: Do what thou darest; I beard thee to thy face.

44.play: GLOUCESTER: What! am I dared and bearded to my face?

45.play: Draw, men, for all this privileged place;

46.play: Blue coats to tawny coats. Priest, beware your beard,

47.play: I mean to tug it and to cuff you soundly:

48.play: Under my feet I stamp thy cardinal's hat:

49.play: In spite of pope or dignities of church,

50.play: Here by the cheeks I'll drag thee up and down.

51.play: BISHOP

52.play: OF WINCHESTER: Gloucester, thou wilt answer this before the pope.

53.play: GLOUCESTER: Winchester goose, I cry, a rope! a rope!

54.play: Now beat them hence; why do you let them stay?

55.play: Thee I'll chase hence, thou wolf in sheep's array.

56.play: Out, tawny coats! out, scarlet hypocrite!

57.play: Peace, mayor! thou know'st little of my wrongs:

58.play: Here's Beaufort, that regards nor God nor king,

59.play: Hath here distrain'd the Tower to his use.

61.play: OF WINCHESTER: Here's Gloucester, a foe to citizens,

62.play: One that still motions war and never peace,

63.play: O'ercharging your free purses with large fines,

65.play: Because he is protector of the realm,

66.play: And would have armour here out of the Tower,

67.play: To crown himself king and suppress the prince.

68.play: GLOUCESTER: I will not answer thee with words, but blows.

69.play: Cardinal, I'll be no breaker of the law:

70.play: But we shall meet, and break our minds at large.

72.play: OF WINCHESTER: Gloucester, we will meet; to thy cost, be sure:

73.play: Thy heart-blood I will have for this day's work.

74.play: GLOUCESTER: Mayor, farewell: thou dost but what thou mayst.

76.play: OF WINCHESTER: Abominable Gloucester, guard thy head;

77.play: For I intend to have it ere long.

1.play: [Enter, on the walls, a Master Gunner and his Boy]

2.play: [Exit]

3.play: [Exit]

4.play: SALISBURY: Talbot, my life, my joy, again return'd!

5.play: How wert thou handled being prisoner?

6.play: Or by what means got'st thou to be released?

7.play: Discourse, I prithee, on this turret's top.

8.play: TALBOT: The Duke of Bedford had a prisoner

9.play: Call'd the brave Lord Ponton de Santrailles;

10.play: For him was I exchanged and ransomed.

11.play: But with a baser man of arms by far

12.play: Once in contempt they would have barter'd me:

13.play: Which I, disdaining, scorn'd; and craved death,

14.play: Rather than I would be so vile esteem'd.

15.play: In fine, redeem'd I was as I desired.

16.play: But, O! the treacherous Fastolfe wounds my heart,

17.play: Whom with my bare fists I would execute,

18.play: If I now had him brought into my power.

19.play: SALISBURY: Yet tell'st thou not how thou wert entertain'd.

20.play: TALBOT: With scoffs and scorns and contumelious taunts.

21.play: In open market-place produced they me,

22.play: To be a public spectacle to all:

23.play: Here, said they, is the terror of the French,

24.play: The scarecrow that affrights our children so.

25.play: Then broke I from the officers that led me,

26.play: And with my nails digg'd stones out of the ground,

27.play: To hurl at the beholders of my shame:

28.play: My grisly countenance made others fly;

29.play: None durst come near for fear of sudden death.

30.play: In iron walls they deem'd me not secure;

31.play: So great fear of my name 'mongst them was spread,

32.play: That they supposed I could rend bars of steel,

33.play: And spurn in pieces posts of adamant:

34.play: Wherefore a guard of chosen shot I had,

35.play: That walked about me every minute-while;

36.play: And if I did but stir out of my bed,

37.play: Ready they were to shoot me to the heart.

38.play: SALISBURY: I grieve to hear what torments you endured,

39.play: But we will be revenged sufficiently

40.play: Now it is supper-time in Orleans:

41.play: Here, through this grate, I count each one

42.play: and view the Frenchmen how they fortify:

43.play: Let us look in; the sight will much delight thee.

44.play: Sir Thomas Gargrave, and Sir William Glansdale,

45.play: Let me have your express opinions

46.play: Where is best place to make our battery next.

47.play: GARGRAVE: I think, at the north gate; for there stand lords.

48.play: GLANSDALE: And I, here, at the bulwark of the bridge.

49.play: TALBOT: For aught I see, this city must be famish'd,

50.play: Or with light skirmishes enfeebled.

51.play: SALISBURY: O Lord, have mercy on us, wretched sinners!

52.play: GARGRAVE: O Lord, have mercy on me, woful man!

53.play: TALBOT: What chance is this that suddenly hath cross'd us?

54.play: Speak, Salisbury; at least, if thou canst speak:

55.play: How farest thou, mirror of all martial men?

56.play: One of thy eyes and thy cheek's side struck off!

58.play: That hath contrived this woful tragedy!

60.play: Henry the Fifth he first train'd to the wars;

61.play: Whilst any trump did sound, or drum struck up,

62.play: His sword did ne'er leave striking in the field.

63.play: Yet livest thou, Salisbury? though thy speech doth fail,

64.play: One eye thou hast, to look to heaven for grace:

65.play: The sun with one eye vieweth all the world.

66.play: Heaven, be thou gracious to none alive,

67.play: If Salisbury wants mercy at thy hands!

68.play: Bear hence his body; I will help to bury it.

69.play: Sir Thomas Gargrave, hast thou any life?

70.play: Speak unto Talbot; nay, look up to him.

71.play: Salisbury, cheer thy spirit with this comfort;

73.play: He beckons with his hand and smiles on me.

74.play: As who should say 'When I am dead and gone,

75.play: Remember to avenge me on the French.'

76.play: Plantagenet, I will; and like thee, Nero,

77.play: Play on the lute, beholding the towns burn:

78.play: Wretched shall France be only in my name.

79.play: What stir is this? what tumult's in the heavens?

80.play: Whence cometh this alarum and the noise?

81.play: Hear, hear how dying Salisbury doth groan!

82.play: It irks his heart he cannot be revenged.

83.play: Frenchmen, I'll be a Salisbury to you:

84.play: Pucelle or puzzel, dolphin or dogfish,

85.play: Your hearts I'll stamp out with my horse's heels,

86.play: And make a quagmire of your mingled brains.

87.play: Convey me Salisbury into his tent,

88.play: And then we'll try what these dastard Frenchmen dare.

1.play: TALBOT: Where is my strength, my valour, and my force?

2.play: Our English troops retire, I cannot stay them:

3.play: A woman clad in armour chaseth them.

4.play: Here, here she comes. I'll have a bout with thee;

5.play: Devil or devil's dam, I'll conjure thee:

6.play: Blood will I draw on thee, thou art a witch,

7.play: And straightway give thy soul to him thou servest.

8.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: Come, come, 'tis only I that must disgrace thee.

9.play: TALBOT: Heavens, can you suffer hell so to prevail?

10.play: My breast I'll burst with straining of my courage

11.play: And from my shoulders crack my arms asunder.

12.play: But I will chastise this high-minded strumpet.

13.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: Talbot, farewell; thy hour is not yet come:

14.play: I must go victual Orleans forthwith.

15.play: O'ertake me, if thou canst; I scorn thy strength.

16.play: Go, go, cheer up thy hungry-starved men;

17.play: Help Salisbury to make his testament:

18.play: This day is ours, as many more shall be.

19.play: TALBOT: My thoughts are whirled like a potter's wheel;

20.play: I know not where I am, nor what I do;

21.play: A witch, by fear, not force, like Hannibal,

22.play: Drives back our troops and conquers as she lists:

23.play: So bees with smoke and doves with noisome stench

24.play: Are from their hives and houses driven away.

25.play: They call'd us for our fierceness English dogs;

26.play: Now, like to whelps, we crying run away.

27.play: Hark, countrymen! either renew the fight,

28.play: Or tear the lions out of England's coat;

29.play: Renounce your soil, give sheep in lions' stead:

30.play: Sheep run not half so treacherous from the wolf,

31.play: Or horse or oxen from the leopard,

32.play: As you fly from your oft-subdued slaves.

33.play: It will not be: retire into your trenches:

34.play: You all consented unto Salisbury's death,

35.play: For none would strike a stroke in his revenge.

36.play: Pucelle is enter'd into Orleans,

37.play: In spite of us or aught that we could do.

38.play: O, would I were to die with Salisbury!

39.play: The shame hereof will make me hide my head.

1.play: [Enter, on the walls, JOAN LA PUCELLE, CHARLES, REIGNIER, ALENCON, and Soldiers]

2.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: Advance our waving colours on the walls;

3.play: Rescued is Orleans from the English

4.play: Thus Joan la Pucelle hath perform'd her word.

5.play: CHARLES: Divinest creature, Astraea's daughter,

6.play: How shall I honour thee for this success?

7.play: Thy promises are like Adonis' gardens

8.play: That one day bloom'd and fruitful were the next.

9.play: France, triumph in thy glorious prophetess!

10.play: Recover'd is the town of Orleans:

11.play: More blessed hap did ne'er befall our state.

12.play: REIGNIER: Why ring not out the bells aloud throughout the town?

13.play: Dauphin, command the citizens make bonfires

14.play: And feast and banquet in the open streets,

15.play: To celebrate the joy that God hath given us.

16.play: ALENCON: All France will be replete with mirth and joy,

17.play: When they shall hear how we have play'd the men.

18.play: CHARLES: 'Tis Joan, not we, by whom the day is won;

19.play: For which I will divide my crown with her,

20.play: And all the priests and friars in my realm

21.play: Shall in procession sing her endless praise.

22.play: A statelier pyramis to her I'll rear

23.play: Than Rhodope's or Memphis' ever was:

24.play: In memory of her when she is dead,

25.play: Her ashes, in an urn more precious

26.play: Than the rich-jewel'd of Darius,

27.play: Transported shall be at high festivals

28.play: Before the kings and queens of France.

29.play: No longer on Saint Denis will we cry,

30.play: But Joan la Pucelle shall be France's saint.

31.play: Come in, and let us banquet royally,

32.play: After this golden day of victory.

33.play: Shakespeare homepage

34.play: Henry VI, part 1

35.play: Previous scene

36.play: Next scene

1.play: [Enter a Sergeant of a band with two Sentinels]

2.play: [Exit Sergeant]

3.play: TALBOT: Lord Regent, and redoubted Burgundy,

4.play: By whose approach the regions of Artois,

5.play: Wallon and Picardy are friends to us,

6.play: This happy night the Frenchmen are secure,

7.play: Having all day caroused and banqueted:

8.play: Embrace we then this opportunity

9.play: As fitting best to quittance their deceit

10.play: Contrived by art and baleful sorcery.

11.play: BEDFORD: Coward of France! how much he wrongs his fame,

12.play: Despairing of his own arm's fortitude,

13.play: To join with witches and the help of hell!

14.play: BURGUNDY: Traitors have never other company.

15.play: But what's that Pucelle whom they term so pure?

16.play: TALBOT: A maid, they say.

17.play: BEDFORD: A maid! and be so martial!

18.play: BURGUNDY: Pray God she prove not masculine ere long,

19.play: If underneath the standard of the French

20.play: She carry armour as she hath begun.

21.play: TALBOT: Well, let them practise and converse with spirits:

22.play: God is our fortress, in whose conquering name

23.play: Let us resolve to scale their flinty bulwarks.

24.play: BEDFORD: Ascend, brave Talbot; we will follow thee.

25.play: TALBOT: Not all together: better far, I guess,

26.play: That we do make our entrance several ways;

27.play: That, if it chance the one of us do fail,

28.play: The other yet may rise against their force.

29.play: BEDFORD: Agreed: I'll to yond corner.

30.play: BURGUNDY: And I to this.

31.play: TALBOT: And here will Talbot mount, or make his grave.

32.play: Now, Salisbury, for thee, and for the right

33.play: Of English Henry, shall this night appear

34.play: How much in duty I am bound to both.

35.play: ALENCON: How now, my lords! what, all unready so?

36.play: BASTARD OF ORLEANS: Unready! ay, and glad we 'scaped so well.

37.play: REIGNIER: 'Twas time, I trow, to wake and leave our beds,

38.play: Hearing alarums at our chamber-doors.

39.play: ALENCON: Of all exploits since first I follow'd arms,

40.play: Ne'er heard I of a warlike enterprise

41.play: More venturous or desperate than this.

42.play: BASTARD OF ORLEANS: I think this Talbot be a fiend of hell.

43.play: REIGNIER: If not of hell, the heavens, sure, favour him.

44.play: ALENCON: Here cometh Charles: I marvel how he sped.

45.play: BASTARD OF ORLEANS: Tut, holy Joan was his defensive guard.

46.play: CHARLES: Is this thy cunning, thou deceitful dame?

47.play: Didst thou at first, to flatter us withal,

48.play: Make us partakers of a little gain,

49.play: That now our loss might be ten times so much?

50.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: Wherefore is Charles impatient with his friend!

51.play: At all times will you have my power alike?

52.play: Sleeping or waking must I still prevail,

53.play: Or will you blame and lay the fault on me?

54.play: Improvident soldiers! had your watch been good,

55.play: This sudden mischief never could have fall'n.

56.play: CHARLES: Duke of Alencon, this was your default,

57.play: That, being captain of the watch to-night,

58.play: Did look no better to that weighty charge.

59.play: ALENCON: Had all your quarters been as safely kept

60.play: As that whereof I had the government,

61.play: We had not been thus shamefully surprised.

62.play: BASTARD OF ORLEANS: Mine was secure.

63.play: REIGNIER: And so was mine, my lord.

64.play: CHARLES: And, for myself, most part of all this night,

65.play: Within her quarter and mine own precinct

66.play: I was employ'd in passing to and fro,

68.play: Then how or which way should they first break in?

69.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: Question, my lords, no further of the case,

70.play: How or which way: 'tis sure they found some place

71.play: But weakly guarded, where the breach was made.

72.play: And now there rests no other shift but this;

73.play: To gather our soldiers, scatter'd and dispersed,

74.play: And lay new platforms to endamage them.

1.play: [Enter TALBOT, BEDFORD, BURGUNDY, a Captain, and others]

2.play: BEDFORD: The day begins to break, and night is fled,

3.play: Whose pitchy mantle over-veil'd the earth.

4.play: Here sound retreat, and cease our hot pursuit.

5.play: TALBOT: Bring forth the body of old Salisbury,

6.play: And here advance it in the market-place,

7.play: The middle centre of this cursed town.

8.play: Now have I paid my vow unto his soul;

9.play: For every drop of blood was drawn from him,

10.play: There hath at least five Frenchmen died tonight.

11.play: And that hereafter ages may behold

12.play: What ruin happen'd in revenge of him,

13.play: Within their chiefest temple I'll erect

14.play: [A tomb, wherein his corpse shall be interr'd:]

15.play: Upon the which, that every one may read,

16.play: Shall be engraved the sack of Orleans,

17.play: The treacherous manner of his mournful death

18.play: And what a terror he had been to France.

19.play: But, lords, in all our bloody massacre,

20.play: I muse we met not with the Dauphin's grace,

21.play: His new-come champion, virtuous Joan of Arc,

22.play: Nor any of his false confederates.

23.play: BEDFORD: 'Tis thought, Lord Talbot, when the fight began,

24.play: Roused on the sudden from their drowsy beds,

25.play: They did amongst the troops of armed men

26.play: Leap o'er the walls for refuge in the field.

27.play: BURGUNDY: Myself, as far as I could well discern

28.play: For smoke and dusky vapours of the night,

29.play: Am sure I scared the Dauphin and his trull,

30.play: When arm in arm they both came swiftly running,

31.play: Like to a pair of loving turtle-doves

32.play: That could not live asunder day or night.

33.play: After that things are set in order here,

34.play: We'll follow them with all the power we have.

35.play: TALBOT: Here is the Talbot: who would speak with him?

36.play: BURGUNDY: Is it even so? Nay, then, I see our wars

37.play: Will turn unto a peaceful comic sport,

38.play: When ladies crave to be encounter'd with.

39.play: You may not, my lord, despise her gentle suit.

40.play: TALBOT: Ne'er trust me then; for when a world of men

41.play: Could not prevail with all their oratory,

42.play: Yet hath a woman's kindness over-ruled:

43.play: And therefore tell her I return great thanks,

44.play: And in submission will attend on her.

45.play: Will not your honours bear me company?

46.play: BEDFORD: No, truly; it is more than manners will:

47.play: And I have heard it said, unbidden guests

48.play: Are often welcomest when they are gone.

49.play: TALBOT: Well then, alone, since there's no remedy,

50.play: I mean to prove this lady's courtesy.

51.play: Come hither, captain.

52.play: You perceive my mind?

53.play: [Exeunt]

1.play: [Enter the COUNTESS and her Porter]

2.play: OF AUVERGNE: Porter, remember what I gave in charge;

3.play: And when you have done so, bring the keys to me.

4.play: [Exit]

5.play: OF AUVERGNE: The plot is laid: if all things fall out right,

6.play: I shall as famous be by this exploit

7.play: As Scythian Tomyris by Cyrus' death.

8.play: Great is the rumor of this dreadful knight,

9.play: And his achievements of no less account:

10.play: Fain would mine eyes be witness with mine ears,

11.play: To give their censure of these rare reports.

12.play: And he is welcome. What! is this the man?

13.play: Is this the scourge of France?

14.play: Is this the Talbot, so much fear'd abroad

15.play: That with his name the mothers still their babes?

16.play: I see report is fabulous and false:

17.play: I thought I should have seen some Hercules,

18.play: A second Hector, for his grim aspect,

19.play: And large proportion of his strong-knit limbs.

20.play: Alas, this is a child, a silly dwarf!

21.play: It cannot be this weak and writhled shrimp

22.play: Should strike such terror to his enemies.

23.play: TALBOT: Madam, I have been bold to trouble you;

24.play: But since your ladyship is not at leisure,

25.play: I'll sort some other time to visit you.

26.play: COUNTESS

27.play: OF AUVERGNE: What means he now? Go ask him whither he goes.

28.play: TALBOT: Marry, for that she's in a wrong belief,

29.play: I go to certify her Talbot's here.

30.play: COUNTESS

31.play: OF AUVERGNE: If thou be he, then art thou prisoner.

32.play: TALBOT: Prisoner! to whom?

33.play: COUNTESS

34.play: OF AUVERGNE: To me, blood-thirsty lord;

35.play: And for that cause I trained thee to my house.

36.play: Long time thy shadow hath been thrall to me,

37.play: For in my gallery thy picture hangs:

38.play: But now the substance shall endure the like,

39.play: And I will chain these legs and arms of thine,

40.play: That hast by tyranny these many years

41.play: Wasted our country, slain our citizens

42.play: And sent our sons and husbands captivate.

43.play: TALBOT: Ha, ha, ha!

44.play: COUNTESS

45.play: OF AUVERGNE: Laughest thou, wretch? thy mirth shall turn to moan.

46.play: TALBOT: I laugh to see your ladyship so fond

47.play: To think that you have aught but Talbot's shadow

48.play: Whereon to practise your severity.

49.play: COUNTESS

50.play: OF AUVERGNE: Why, art not thou the man?

51.play: TALBOT: I am indeed.

52.play: COUNTESS

53.play: OF AUVERGNE: Then have I substance too.

54.play: TALBOT: No, no, I am but shadow of myself:

55.play: You are deceived, my substance is not here;

56.play: For what you see is but the smallest part

58.play: I tell you, madam, were the whole frame here,

59.play: It is of such a spacious lofty pitch,

60.play: Your roof were not sufficient to contain't.

62.play: OF AUVERGNE: This is a riddling merchant for the nonce;

63.play: He will be here, and yet he is not here:

65.play: TALBOT: That will I show you presently.

66.play: How say you, madam? are you now persuaded

67.play: That Talbot is but shadow of himself?

68.play: These are his substance, sinews, arms and strength,

69.play: With which he yoketh your rebellious necks,

70.play: Razeth your cities and subverts your towns

71.play: And in a moment makes them desolate.

73.play: OF AUVERGNE: Victorious Talbot! pardon my abuse:

74.play: I find thou art no less than fame hath bruited

75.play: And more than may be gather'd by thy shape.

76.play: Let my presumption not provoke thy wrath;

77.play: For I am sorry that with reverence

78.play: I did not entertain thee as thou art.

79.play: TALBOT: Be not dismay'd, fair lady; nor misconstrue

80.play: The mind of Talbot, as you did mistake

81.play: The outward composition of his body.

82.play: What you have done hath not offended me;

83.play: Nor other satisfaction do I crave,

84.play: But only, with your patience, that we may

85.play: Taste of your wine and see what cates you have;

86.play: For soldiers' stomachs always serve them well.

88.play: OF AUVERGNE: With all my heart, and think me honoured

89.play: To feast so great a warrior in my house.

1.play: [Enter the Earls of SOMERSET, SUFFOLK, and WARWICK; RICHARD PLANTAGENET, VERNON, and another Lawyer]

2.play: PLANTAGENET: Great lords and gentlemen, what means this silence?

3.play: Dare no man answer in a case of truth?

4.play: SUFFOLK: Within the Temple-hall we were too loud;

5.play: The garden here is more convenient.

6.play: RICHARD

7.play: PLANTAGENET: Then say at once if I maintain'd the truth;

8.play: Or else was wrangling Somerset in the error?

9.play: SUFFOLK: Faith, I have been a truant in the law,

10.play: And never yet could frame my will to it;

11.play: And therefore frame the law unto my will.

12.play: SOMERSET: Judge you, my Lord of Warwick, then, between us.

13.play: WARWICK: Between two hawks, which flies the higher pitch;

14.play: Between two dogs, which hath the deeper mouth;

15.play: Between two blades, which bears the better temper:

16.play: Between two horses, which doth bear him best;

17.play: Between two girls, which hath the merriest eye;

18.play: I have perhaps some shallow spirit of judgement;

19.play: But in these nice sharp quillets of the law,

20.play: Good faith, I am no wiser than a daw.

21.play: RICHARD

22.play: PLANTAGENET: Tut, tut, here is a mannerly forbearance:

23.play: The truth appears so naked on my side

24.play: That any purblind eye may find it out.

25.play: SOMERSET: And on my side it is so well apparell'd,

26.play: So clear, so shining and so evident

27.play: That it will glimmer through a blind man's eye.

28.play: RICHARD

29.play: PLANTAGENET: Since you are tongue-tied and so loath to speak,

30.play: In dumb significants proclaim your thoughts:

31.play: Let him that is a true-born gentleman

32.play: And stands upon the honour of his birth,

33.play: If he suppose that I have pleaded truth,

34.play: From off this brier pluck a white rose with me.

35.play: SOMERSET: Let him that is no coward nor no flatterer,

36.play: But dare maintain the party of the truth,

37.play: Pluck a red rose from off this thorn with me.

38.play: WARWICK: I love no colours, and without all colour

39.play: Of base insinuating flattery

40.play: I pluck this white rose with Plantagenet.

41.play: SUFFOLK: I pluck this red rose with young Somerset

42.play: And say withal I think he held the right.

43.play: VERNON: Stay, lords and gentlemen, and pluck no more,

44.play: Till you conclude that he upon whose side

45.play: The fewest roses are cropp'd from the tree

46.play: Shall yield the other in the right opinion.

47.play: SOMERSET: Good Master Vernon, it is well objected:

48.play: If I have fewest, I subscribe in silence.

49.play: RICHARD

50.play: PLANTAGENET: And I.

51.play: VERNON: Then for the truth and plainness of the case.

52.play: I pluck this pale and maiden blossom here,

53.play: Giving my verdict on the white rose side.

54.play: SOMERSET: Prick not your finger as you pluck it off,

55.play: Lest bleeding you do paint the white rose red

56.play: And fall on my side so, against your will.

57.play: VERNON: If I my lord, for my opinion bleed,

58.play: Opinion shall be surgeon to my hurt

59.play: And keep me on the side where still I am.

60.play: SOMERSET: Well, well, come on: who else?

61.play: PLANTAGENET: Now, Somerset, where is your argument?

62.play: SOMERSET: Here in my scabbard, meditating that

63.play: Shall dye your white rose in a bloody red.

65.play: PLANTAGENET: Meantime your cheeks do counterfeit our roses;

66.play: For pale they look with fear, as witnessing

69.play: 'Tis not for fear but anger that thy cheeks

70.play: Blush for pure shame to counterfeit our roses,

71.play: And yet thy tongue will not confess thy error.

73.play: PLANTAGENET: Hath not thy rose a canker, Somerset?

74.play: SOMERSET: Hath not thy rose a thorn, Plantagenet?

76.play: PLANTAGENET: Ay, sharp and piercing, to maintain his truth;

77.play: Whiles thy consuming canker eats his falsehood.

78.play: SOMERSET: Well, I'll find friends to wear my bleeding roses,

79.play: That shall maintain what I have said is true,

80.play: Where false Plantagenet dare not be seen.

82.play: PLANTAGENET: Now, by this maiden blossom in my hand,

83.play: I scorn thee and thy fashion, peevish boy.

84.play: SUFFOLK: Turn not thy scorns this way, Plantagenet.

86.play: PLANTAGENET: Proud Pole, I will, and scorn both him and thee.

87.play: SUFFOLK: I'll turn my part thereof into thy throat.

88.play: SOMERSET: Away, away, good William de la Pole!

89.play: We grace the yeoman by conversing with him.

90.play: WARWICK: Now, by God's will, thou wrong'st him, Somerset;

91.play: His grandfather was Lionel Duke of Clarence,

92.play: Third son to the third Edward King of England:

93.play: Spring crestless yeomen from so deep a root?

95.play: PLANTAGENET: He bears him on the place's privilege,

96.play: Or durst not, for his craven heart, say thus.

97.play: SOMERSET: By him that made me, I'll maintain my words

98.play: On any plot of ground in Christendom.

99.play: Was not thy father, Richard Earl of Cambridge,

100.play: For treason executed in our late king's days?

101.play: And, by his treason, stand'st not thou attainted,

102.play: Corrupted, and exempt from ancient gentry?

103.play: His trespass yet lives guilty in thy blood;

104.play: And, till thou be restored, thou art a yeoman.

106.play: PLANTAGENET: My father was attached, not attainted,

107.play: Condemn'd to die for treason, but no traitor;

108.play: And that I'll prove on better men than Somerset,

109.play: Were growing time once ripen'd to my will.

110.play: For your partaker Pole and you yourself,

111.play: I'll note you in my book of memory,

112.play: To scourge you for this apprehension:

113.play: Look to it well and say you are well warn'd.

114.play: SOMERSET: Ah, thou shalt find us ready for thee still;

115.play: And know us by these colours for thy foes,

116.play: For these my friends in spite of thee shall wear.

118.play: PLANTAGENET: And, by my soul, this pale and angry rose,

119.play: As cognizance of my blood-drinking hate,

120.play: Will I for ever and my faction wear,

121.play: Until it wither with me to my grave

122.play: Or flourish to the height of my degree.

123.play: SUFFOLK: Go forward and be choked with thy ambition!

124.play: And so farewell until I meet thee next.

125.play: SOMERSET: Have with thee, Pole. Farewell, ambitious Richard.

127.play: PLANTAGENET: How I am braved and must perforce endure it!

128.play: WARWICK: This blot that they object against your house

129.play: Shall be wiped out in the next parliament

130.play: Call'd for the truce of Winchester and Gloucester;

131.play: And if thou be not then created York,

132.play: I will not live to be accounted Warwick.

133.play: Meantime, in signal of my love to thee,

134.play: Against proud Somerset and William Pole,

135.play: Will I upon thy party wear this rose:

136.play: And here I prophesy: this brawl to-day,

137.play: Grown to this faction in the Temple-garden,

138.play: Shall send between the red rose and the white

139.play: A thousand souls to death and deadly night.

141.play: PLANTAGENET: Good Master Vernon, I am bound to you,

142.play: That you on my behalf would pluck a flower.

143.play: VERNON: In your behalf still will I wear the same.

145.play: Come, let us four to dinner: I dare say

146.play: This quarrel will drink blood another day.

1.play: [Enter MORTIMER, brought in a chair, and Gaolers]

2.play: MORTIMER: Kind keepers of my weak decaying age,

3.play: Let dying Mortimer here rest himself.

4.play: Even like a man new haled from the rack,

5.play: So fare my limbs with long imprisonment.

6.play: And these grey locks, the pursuivants of death,

7.play: Nestor-like aged in an age of care,

8.play: Argue the end of Edmund Mortimer.

9.play: These eyes, like lamps whose wasting oil is spent,

10.play: Wax dim, as drawing to their exigent;

11.play: Weak shoulders, overborne with burthening grief,

12.play: And pithless arms, like to a wither'd vine

13.play: That droops his sapless branches to the ground;

14.play: Yet are these feet, whose strengthless stay is numb,

15.play: Unable to support this lump of clay,

16.play: Swift-winged with desire to get a grave,

17.play: As witting I no other comfort have.

18.play: But tell me, keeper, will my nephew come?

19.play: Enough: my soul shall then be satisfied.

20.play: Poor gentleman! his wrong doth equal mine.

21.play: Since Henry Monmouth first began to reign,

22.play: Before whose glory I was great in arms,

23.play: This loathsome sequestration have I had:

24.play: And even since then hath Richard been obscured,

25.play: Deprived of honour and inheritance.

26.play: But now the arbitrator of despairs,

27.play: Just death, kind umpire of men's miseries,

28.play: With sweet enlargement doth dismiss me hence:

29.play: I would his troubles likewise were expired,

30.play: That so he might recover what was lost.

31.play: Richard Plantagenet, my friend, is he come?

32.play: RICHARD

33.play: PLANTAGENET: Ay, noble uncle, thus ignobly used,

34.play: Your nephew, late despised Richard, comes.

35.play: MORTIMER: Direct mine arms I may embrace his neck,

36.play: And in his bosom spend my latter gasp:

37.play: O, tell me when my lips do touch his cheeks,

38.play: That I may kindly give one fainting kiss.

39.play: And now declare, sweet stem from York's great stock,

40.play: Why didst thou say, of late thou wert despised?

41.play: RICHARD

42.play: PLANTAGENET: First, lean thine aged back against mine arm;

43.play: And, in that ease, I'll tell thee my disease.

44.play: This day, in argument upon a case,

45.play: Some words there grew 'twixt Somerset and me;

46.play: Among which terms he used his lavish tongue

47.play: And did upbraid me with my father's death:

48.play: Which obloquy set bars before my tongue,

49.play: Else with the like I had requited him.

50.play: Therefore, good uncle, for my father's sake,

51.play: In honour of a true Plantagenet

52.play: And for alliance sake, declare the cause

53.play: My father, Earl of Cambridge, lost his head.

54.play: MORTIMER: That cause, fair nephew, that imprison'd me

55.play: And hath detain'd me all my flowering youth

56.play: Within a loathsome dungeon, there to pine,

57.play: Was cursed instrument of his decease.

59.play: PLANTAGENET: Discover more at large what cause that was,

60.play: For I am ignorant and cannot guess.

61.play: MORTIMER: I will, if that my fading breath permit

62.play: And death approach not ere my tale be done.

63.play: Henry the Fourth, grandfather to this king,

64.play: Deposed his nephew Richard, Edward's son,

65.play: The first-begotten and the lawful heir,

66.play: Of Edward king, the third of that descent:

67.play: During whose reign the Percies of the north,

69.play: Endeavor'd my advancement to the throne:

70.play: The reason moved these warlike lords to this

71.play: Was, for that--young King Richard thus removed,

72.play: Leaving no heir begotten of his body--

73.play: I was the next by birth and parentage;

74.play: For by my mother I derived am

75.play: From Lionel Duke of Clarence, the third son

76.play: To King Edward the Third; whereas he

77.play: From John of Gaunt doth bring his pedigree,

78.play: Being but fourth of that heroic line.

79.play: But mark: as in this haughty attempt

80.play: They laboured to plant the rightful heir,

81.play: I lost my liberty and they their lives.

82.play: Long after this, when Henry the Fifth,

83.play: Succeeding his father Bolingbroke, did reign,

84.play: Thy father, Earl of Cambridge, then derived

85.play: From famous Edmund Langley, Duke of York,

86.play: Marrying my sister that thy mother was,

87.play: Again in pity of my hard distress

88.play: Levied an army, weening to redeem

89.play: And have install'd me in the diadem:

90.play: But, as the rest, so fell that noble earl

91.play: And was beheaded. Thus the Mortimers,

92.play: In whom the tide rested, were suppress'd.

94.play: PLANTAGENET: Of which, my lord, your honour is the last.

95.play: MORTIMER: True; and thou seest that I no issue have

96.play: And that my fainting words do warrant death;

97.play: Thou art my heir; the rest I wish thee gather:

98.play: But yet be wary in thy studious care.

100.play: PLANTAGENET: Thy grave admonishments prevail with me:

101.play: But yet, methinks, my father's execution

102.play: Was nothing less than bloody tyranny.

103.play: MORTIMER: With silence, nephew, be thou politic:

104.play: Strong-fixed is the house of Lancaster,

105.play: And like a mountain, not to be removed.

106.play: But now thy uncle is removing hence:

107.play: As princes do their courts, when they are cloy'd

108.play: With long continuance in a settled place.

110.play: PLANTAGENET: O, uncle, would some part of my young years

111.play: Might but redeem the passage of your age!

112.play: MORTIMER: Thou dost then wrong me, as that slaughterer doth

113.play: Which giveth many wounds when one will kill.

114.play: Mourn not, except thou sorrow for my good;

115.play: Only give order for my funeral:

116.play: And so farewell, and fair be all thy hopes

117.play: And prosperous be thy life in peace and war!

119.play: PLANTAGENET: And peace, no war, befall thy parting soul!

120.play: In prison hast thou spent a pilgrimage

121.play: And like a hermit overpass'd thy days.

122.play: Well, I will lock his counsel in my breast;

123.play: And what I do imagine let that rest.

124.play: Keepers, convey him hence, and I myself

125.play: Will see his burial better than his life.

126.play: Here dies the dusky torch of Mortimer,

127.play: Choked with ambition of the meaner sort:

128.play: And for those wrongs, those bitter injuries,

129.play: Which Somerset hath offer'd to my house:

130.play: I doubt not but with honour to redress;

131.play: And therefore haste I to the parliament,

132.play: Either to be restored to my blood,

133.play: Or make my ill the advantage of my good.

1.play: [Flourish. Enter KING HENRY VI, EXETER, GLOUCESTER,  WARWICK, SOMERSET, and SUFFOLK; the BISHOP OF  WINCHESTER, RICHARD PLANTAGENET, and others.  GLOUCESTER offers to put up a bill; BISHOP OF WINCHESTER snatches it, and tears it]

2.play: OF WINCHESTER: Comest thou with deep premeditated lines,

3.play: With written pamphlets studiously devised,

4.play: Humphrey of Gloucester? If thou canst accuse,

5.play: Or aught intend'st to lay unto my charge,

6.play: Do it without invention, suddenly;

7.play: As I with sudden and extemporal speech

8.play: Purpose to answer what thou canst object.

9.play: GLOUCESTER: Presumptuous priest! this place commands my patience,

10.play: Or thou shouldst find thou hast dishonour'd me.

11.play: Think not, although in writing I preferr'd

12.play: The manner of thy vile outrageous crimes,

13.play: That therefore I have forged, or am not able

14.play: Verbatim to rehearse the method of my pen:

15.play: No, prelate; such is thy audacious wickedness,

16.play: Thy lewd, pestiferous and dissentious pranks,

17.play: As very infants prattle of thy pride.

18.play: Thou art a most pernicious usurer,

19.play: Forward by nature, enemy to peace;

20.play: Lascivious, wanton, more than well beseems

21.play: A man of thy profession and degree;

22.play: And for thy treachery, what's more manifest?

23.play: In that thou laid'st a trap to take my life,

24.play: As well at London bridge as at the Tower.

25.play: Beside, I fear me, if thy thoughts were sifted,

26.play: The king, thy sovereign, is not quite exempt

27.play: From envious malice of thy swelling heart.

28.play: BISHOP

29.play: OF WINCHESTER: Gloucester, I do defy thee. Lords, vouchsafe

30.play: To give me hearing what I shall reply.

31.play: If I were covetous, ambitious or perverse,

32.play: As he will have me, how am I so poor?

33.play: Or how haps it I seek not to advance

34.play: Or raise myself, but keep my wonted calling?

35.play: And for dissension, who preferreth peace

36.play: More than I do?--except I be provoked.

37.play: No, my good lords, it is not that offends;

38.play: It is not that that hath incensed the duke:

39.play: It is, because no one should sway but he;

40.play: No one but he should be about the king;

41.play: And that engenders thunder in his breast

42.play: And makes him roar these accusations forth.

43.play: But he shall know I am as good--

44.play: GLOUCESTER: As good!

45.play: Thou bastard of my grandfather!

46.play: BISHOP

47.play: OF WINCHESTER: Ay, lordly sir; for what are you, I pray,

48.play: But one imperious in another's throne?

49.play: GLOUCESTER: Am I not protector, saucy priest?

50.play: BISHOP

51.play: OF WINCHESTER: And am not I a prelate of the church?

52.play: GLOUCESTER: Yes, as an outlaw in a castle keeps

53.play: And useth it to patronage his theft.

57.play: Touching thy spiritual function, not thy life.

59.play: OF WINCHESTER: Rome shall remedy this.

61.play: SOMERSET: My lord, it were your duty to forbear.

62.play: WARWICK: Ay, see the bishop be not overborne.

63.play: SOMERSET: Methinks my lord should be religious

64.play: And know the office that belongs to such.

65.play: WARWICK: Methinks his lordship should be humbler;

66.play: it fitteth not a prelate so to plead.

67.play: SOMERSET: Yes, when his holy state is touch'd so near.

68.play: WARWICK: State holy or unhallow'd, what of that?

69.play: Is not his grace protector to the king?

71.play: PLANTAGENET: [Aside]  Plantagenet, I see, must hold his tongue,

72.play: Lest it be said 'Speak, sirrah, when you should;

73.play: Must your bold verdict enter talk with lords?'

74.play: Else would I have a fling at Winchester.

75.play: KING HENRY VI: Uncles of Gloucester and of Winchester,

76.play: The special watchmen of our English weal,

77.play: I would prevail, if prayers might prevail,

78.play: To join your hearts in love and amity.

79.play: O, what a scandal is it to our crown,

80.play: That two such noble peers as ye should jar!

81.play: Believe me, lords, my tender years can tell

82.play: Civil dissension is a viperous worm

83.play: That gnaws the bowels of the commonwealth.

85.play: WARWICK: An uproar, I dare warrant,

86.play: Begun through malice of the bishop's men.

87.play: [Enter Serving-men, in skirmish, with bloody pates]

88.play: KING HENRY VI: We charge you, on allegiance to ourself,

89.play: To hold your slaughtering hands and keep the peace.

90.play: Pray, uncle Gloucester, mitigate this strife.

91.play: First Serving-man	Nay, if we be forbidden stones,

92.play: We'll fall to it with our teeth.

93.play: Second Serving-man	Do what ye dare, we are as resolute.

94.play: GLOUCESTER: You of my household, leave this peevish broil

95.play: And set this unaccustom'd fight aside.

96.play: Third Serving-man	My lord, we know your grace to be a man

97.play: Just and upright; and, for your royal birth,

98.play: Inferior to none but to his majesty:

99.play: And ere that we will suffer such a prince,

100.play: So kind a father of the commonweal,

101.play: To be disgraced by an inkhorn mate,

102.play: We and our wives and children all will fight

103.play: And have our bodies slaughtered by thy foes.

104.play: First Serving-man	Ay, and the very parings of our nails

105.play: Shall pitch a field when we are dead.

107.play: And if you love me, as you say you do,

108.play: Let me persuade you to forbear awhile.

109.play: KING HENRY VI: O, how this discord doth afflict my soul!

110.play: Can you, my Lord of Winchester, behold

111.play: My sighs and tears and will not once relent?

112.play: Who should be pitiful, if you be not?

113.play: Or who should study to prefer a peace.

114.play: If holy churchmen take delight in broils?

115.play: WARWICK: Yield, my lord protector; yield, Winchester;

116.play: Except you mean with obstinate repulse

117.play: To slay your sovereign and destroy the realm.

118.play: You see what mischief and what murder too

119.play: Hath been enacted through your enmity;

120.play: Then be at peace except ye thirst for blood.

122.play: OF WINCHESTER: He shall submit, or I will never yield.

123.play: GLOUCESTER: Compassion on the king commands me stoop;

124.play: Or I would see his heart out, ere the priest

125.play: Should ever get that privilege of me.

126.play: WARWICK: Behold, my Lord of Winchester, the duke

128.play: As by his smoothed brows it doth appear:

129.play: Why look you still so stern and tragical?

130.play: GLOUCESTER: Here, Winchester, I offer thee my hand.

131.play: KING HENRY VI: Fie, uncle Beaufort! I have heard you preach

132.play: That malice was a great and grievous sin;

133.play: And will not you maintain the thing you teach,

134.play: But prove a chief offender in the same?

135.play: WARWICK: Sweet king! the bishop hath a kindly gird.

136.play: For shame, my lord of Winchester, relent!

137.play: What, shall a child instruct you what to do?

139.play: OF WINCHESTER: Well, Duke of Gloucester, I will yield to thee;

140.play: Love for thy love and hand for hand I give.

141.play: GLOUCESTER: [Aside]  Ay, but, I fear me, with a hollow heart.--

142.play: See here, my friends and loving countrymen,

143.play: This token serveth for a flag of truce

144.play: Betwixt ourselves and all our followers:

145.play: So help me God, as I dissemble not!

147.play: OF WINCHESTER: [Aside]  So help me God, as I intend it not!

148.play: KING HENRY VI: O, loving uncle, kind Duke of Gloucester,

149.play: How joyful am I made by this contract!

150.play: Away, my masters! trouble us no more;

151.play: But join in friendship, as your lords have done.

152.play: First Serving-man	Content: I'll to the surgeon's.

153.play: Second Serving-man	And so will I.

154.play: Third Serving-man	And I will see what physic the tavern affords.

155.play: WARWICK: Accept this scroll, most gracious sovereign,

156.play: Which in the right of Richard Plantagenet

157.play: We do exhibit to your majesty.

158.play: GLOUCESTER: Well urged, my Lord of Warwick: or sweet prince,

159.play: And if your grace mark every circumstance,

160.play: You have great reason to do Richard right;

162.play: At Eltham Place I told your majesty.

163.play: KING HENRY VI: And those occasions, uncle, were of force:

164.play: Therefore, my loving lords, our pleasure is

165.play: That Richard be restored to his blood.

166.play: WARWICK: Let Richard be restored to his blood;

167.play: So shall his father's wrongs be recompensed.

169.play: OF WINCHESTER: As will the rest, so willeth Winchester.

170.play: KING HENRY VI: If Richard will be true, not that alone

171.play: But all the whole inheritance I give

172.play: That doth belong unto the house of York,

173.play: From whence you spring by lineal descent.

175.play: PLANTAGENET: Thy humble servant vows obedience

176.play: And humble service till the point of death.

177.play: KING HENRY VI: Stoop then and set your knee against my foot;

178.play: And, in reguerdon of that duty done,

179.play: I gird thee with the valiant sword of York:

180.play: Rise Richard, like a true Plantagenet,

181.play: And rise created princely Duke of York.

183.play: PLANTAGENET: And so thrive Richard as thy foes may fall!

184.play: And as my duty springs, so perish they

185.play: That grudge one thought against your majesty!

186.play: ALL: Welcome, high prince, the mighty Duke of York!

187.play: SOMERSET: [Aside]  Perish, base prince, ignoble Duke of York!

188.play: GLOUCESTER: Now will it best avail your majesty

189.play: To cross the seas and to be crown'd in France:

190.play: The presence of a king engenders love

191.play: Amongst his subjects and his loyal friends,

193.play: KING HENRY VI: When Gloucester says the word, King Henry goes;

194.play: For friendly counsel cuts off many foes.

195.play: GLOUCESTER: Your ships already are in readiness.

196.play: EXETER: Ay, we may march in England or in France,

197.play: Not seeing what is likely to ensue.

198.play: This late dissension grown betwixt the peers

199.play: Burns under feigned ashes of forged love

200.play: And will at last break out into a flame:

201.play: As fester'd members rot but by degree,

202.play: Till bones and flesh and sinews fall away,

203.play: So will this base and envious discord breed.

204.play: And now I fear that fatal prophecy

205.play: Which in the time of Henry named the Fifth

206.play: Was in the mouth of every sucking babe;

207.play: That Henry born at Monmouth should win all

208.play: And Henry born at Windsor lose all:

209.play: Which is so plain that Exeter doth wish

210.play: His days may finish ere that hapless time.

1.play: [Enter JOAN LA PUCELLE disguised, with four Soldiers with sacks upon their backs]

2.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: These are the city gates, the gates of Rouen,

3.play: Through which our policy must make a breach:

4.play: Take heed, be wary how you place your words;

5.play: Talk like the vulgar sort of market men

6.play: That come to gather money for their corn.

7.play: If we have entrance, as I hope we shall,

8.play: And that we find the slothful watch but weak,

9.play: I'll by a sign give notice to our friends,

10.play: That Charles the Dauphin may encounter them.

11.play: Paysans, pauvres gens de France;

12.play: Poor market folks that come to sell their corn.

13.play: Now, Rouen, I'll shake thy bulwarks to the ground.

14.play: CHARLES: Saint Denis bless this happy stratagem!

15.play: And once again we'll sleep secure in Rouen.

16.play: BASTARD OF ORLEANS: Here enter'd Pucelle and her practisants;

17.play: Now she is there, how will she specify

18.play: Where is the best and safest passage in?

19.play: REIGNIER: By thrusting out a torch from yonder tower;

20.play: Which, once discern'd, shows that her meaning is,

21.play: No way to that, for weakness, which she enter'd.

22.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: Behold, this is the happy wedding torch

23.play: That joineth Rouen unto her countrymen,

24.play: But burning fatal to the Talbotites!

25.play: BASTARD OF ORLEANS: See, noble Charles, the beacon of our friend;

26.play: The burning torch in yonder turret stands.

27.play: CHARLES: Now shine it like a comet of revenge,

28.play: A prophet to the fall of all our foes!

29.play: REIGNIER: Defer no time, delays have dangerous ends;

30.play: [Enter, and cry 'The Dauphin!' presently, And then do execution on the watch.]

32.play: TALBOT: France, thou shalt rue this treason with thy tears,

33.play: If Talbot but survive thy treachery.

34.play: Pucelle, that witch, that damned sorceress,

35.play: Hath wrought this hellish mischief unawares,

36.play: That hardly we escaped the pride of France.

37.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: Good morrow, gallants! want ye corn for bread?

38.play: I think the Duke of Burgundy will fast

39.play: Before he'll buy again at such a rate:

40.play: 'Twas full of darnel; do you like the taste?

41.play: BURGUNDY: Scoff on, vile fiend and shameless courtezan!

42.play: I trust ere long to choke thee with thine own

43.play: And make thee curse the harvest of that corn.

44.play: CHARLES: Your grace may starve perhaps before that time.

45.play: BEDFORD: O, let no words, but deeds, revenge this treason!

46.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: What will you do, good grey-beard? break a lance,

47.play: And run a tilt at death within a chair?

48.play: TALBOT: Foul fiend of France, and hag of all despite,

49.play: Encompass'd with thy lustful paramours!

50.play: Becomes it thee to taunt his valiant age

51.play: And twit with cowardice a man half dead?

52.play: Damsel, I'll have a bout with you again,

53.play: Or else let Talbot perish with this shame.

54.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: Are ye so hot, sir? yet, Pucelle, hold thy peace;

55.play: If Talbot do but thunder, rain will follow.

56.play: God speed the parliament! who shall be the speaker?

57.play: TALBOT: Dare ye come forth and meet us in the field?

58.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: Belike your lordship takes us then for fools,

59.play: To try if that our own be ours or no.

60.play: TALBOT: I speak not to that railing Hecate,

61.play: But unto thee, Alencon, and the rest;

62.play: Will ye, like soldiers, come and fight it out?

64.play: TALBOT: Signior, hang! base muleters of France!

65.play: Like peasant foot-boys do they keep the walls

66.play: And dare not take up arms like gentlemen.

67.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: Away, captains! let's get us from the walls;

68.play: For Talbot means no goodness by his looks.

69.play: God be wi' you, my lord! we came but to tell you

71.play: TALBOT: And there will we be too, ere it be long,

72.play: Or else reproach be Talbot's greatest fame!

73.play: Vow, Burgundy, by honour of thy house,

74.play: Prick'd on by public wrongs sustain'd in France,

75.play: Either to get the town again or die:

76.play: And I, as sure as English Henry lives

77.play: And as his father here was conqueror,

78.play: As sure as in this late-betrayed town

80.play: So sure I swear to get the town or die.

81.play: BURGUNDY: My vows are equal partners with thy vows.

82.play: TALBOT: But, ere we go, regard this dying prince,

83.play: The valiant Duke of Bedford. Come, my lord,

84.play: We will bestow you in some better place,

85.play: Fitter for sickness and for crazy age.

86.play: BEDFORD: Lord Talbot, do not so dishonour me:

87.play: Here will I sit before the walls of Rouen

88.play: And will be partner of your weal or woe.

89.play: BURGUNDY: Courageous Bedford, let us now persuade you.

90.play: BEDFORD: Not to be gone from hence; for once I read

91.play: That stout Pendragon in his litter sick

92.play: Came to the field and vanquished his foes:

93.play: Methinks I should revive the soldiers' hearts,

94.play: Because I ever found them as myself.

95.play: TALBOT: Undaunted spirit in a dying breast!

96.play: Then be it so: heavens keep old Bedford safe!

97.play: And now no more ado, brave Burgundy,

98.play: But gather we our forces out of hand

99.play: And set upon our boasting enemy.

100.play: FASTOLFE: Whither away! to save myself by flight:

101.play: We are like to have the overthrow again.

103.play: All the Talbots in the world, to save my life!

105.play: BEDFORD: Now, quiet soul, depart when heaven please,

106.play: For I have seen our enemies' overthrow.

107.play: What is the trust or strength of foolish man?

108.play: They that of late were daring with their scoffs

109.play: Are glad and fain by flight to save themselves.

110.play: TALBOT: Lost, and recover'd in a day again!

111.play: This is a double honour, Burgundy:

112.play: Yet heavens have glory for this victory!

113.play: BURGUNDY: Warlike and martial Talbot, Burgundy

114.play: Enshrines thee in his heart and there erects

115.play: Thy noble deeds as valour's monuments.

116.play: TALBOT: Thanks, gentle duke. But where is Pucelle now?

117.play: I think her old familiar is asleep:

118.play: Now where's the Bastard's braves, and Charles his gleeks?

119.play: What, all amort? Rouen hangs her head for grief

120.play: That such a valiant company are fled.

121.play: Now will we take some order in the town,

123.play: And then depart to Paris to the king,

124.play: For there young Henry with his nobles lie.

125.play: BURGUNDY: What wills Lord Talbot pleaseth Burgundy.

126.play: TALBOT: But yet, before we go, let's not forget

127.play: The noble Duke of Bedford late deceased,

128.play: But see his exequies fulfill'd in Rouen:

129.play: A braver soldier never couched lance,

130.play: A gentler heart did never sway in court;

131.play: But kings and mightiest potentates must die,

132.play: For that's the end of human misery.

1.play: [Enter CHARLES, the BASTARD OF ORLEANS, ALENCON, JOAN LA PUCELLE, and forces]

2.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: Dismay not, princes, at this accident,

3.play: Nor grieve that Rouen is so recovered:

4.play: Care is no cure, but rather corrosive,

5.play: For things that are not to be remedied.

6.play: Let frantic Talbot triumph for a while

7.play: And like a peacock sweep along his tail;

8.play: We'll pull his plumes and take away his train,

9.play: If Dauphin and the rest will be but ruled.

10.play: CHARLES: We have been guided by thee hitherto,

11.play: And of thy cunning had no diffidence:

12.play: One sudden foil shall never breed distrust.

13.play: BASTARD OF ORLEANS: Search out thy wit for secret policies,

14.play: And we will make thee famous through the world.

15.play: ALENCON: We'll set thy statue in some holy place,

16.play: And have thee reverenced like a blessed saint:

17.play: Employ thee then, sweet virgin, for our good.

18.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: Then thus it must be; this doth Joan devise:

19.play: By fair persuasions mix'd with sugar'd words

20.play: We will entice the Duke of Burgundy

21.play: To leave the Talbot and to follow us.

22.play: CHARLES: Ay, marry, sweeting, if we could do that,

23.play: France were no place for Henry's warriors;

24.play: Nor should that nation boast it so with us,

25.play: But be extirped from our provinces.

26.play: ALENCON: For ever should they be expulsed from France

27.play: And not have title of an earldom here.

28.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: Your honours shall perceive how I will work

29.play: To bring this matter to the wished end.

30.play: Hark! by the sound of drum you may perceive

31.play: Their powers are marching unto Paris-ward.

32.play: There goes the Talbot, with his colours spread,

33.play: And all the troops of English after him.

34.play: Now in the rearward comes the duke and his:

35.play: Fortune in favour makes him lag behind.

36.play: Summon a parley; we will talk with him.

37.play: CHARLES: A parley with the Duke of Burgundy!

38.play: BURGUNDY: Who craves a parley with the Burgundy?

39.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: The princely Charles of France, thy countryman.

40.play: BURGUNDY: What say'st thou, Charles? for I am marching hence.

41.play: CHARLES: Speak, Pucelle, and enchant him with thy words.

42.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: Brave Burgundy, undoubted hope of France!

43.play: Stay, let thy humble handmaid speak to thee.

44.play: BURGUNDY: Speak on; but be not over-tedious.

45.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: Look on thy country, look on fertile France,

46.play: And see the cities and the towns defaced

47.play: By wasting ruin of the cruel foe.

48.play: As looks the mother on her lowly babe

49.play: When death doth close his tender dying eyes,

50.play: See, see the pining malady of France;

51.play: Behold the wounds, the most unnatural wounds,

52.play: Which thou thyself hast given her woful breast.

53.play: O, turn thy edged sword another way;

54.play: Strike those that hurt, and hurt not those that help.

55.play: One drop of blood drawn from thy country's bosom

56.play: Should grieve thee more than streams of foreign gore:

57.play: Return thee therefore with a flood of tears,

58.play: And wash away thy country's stained spots.

59.play: BURGUNDY: Either she hath bewitch'd me with her words,

60.play: Or nature makes me suddenly relent.

61.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: Besides, all French and France exclaims on thee,

62.play: Doubting thy birth and lawful progeny.

63.play: Who joint'st thou with but with a lordly nation

64.play: That will not trust thee but for profit's sake?

65.play: When Talbot hath set footing once in France

66.play: And fashion'd thee that instrument of ill,

67.play: Who then but English Henry will be lord

68.play: And thou be thrust out like a fugitive?

69.play: Call we to mind, and mark but this for proof,

70.play: Was not the Duke of Orleans thy foe?

71.play: And was he not in England prisoner?

72.play: But when they heard he was thine enemy,

73.play: They set him free without his ransom paid,

74.play: In spite of Burgundy and all his friends.

75.play: See, then, thou fight'st against thy countrymen

76.play: And joint'st with them will be thy slaughtermen.

77.play: Come, come, return; return, thou wandering lord:

78.play: Charles and the rest will take thee in their arms.

79.play: BURGUNDY: I am vanquished; these haughty words of hers

80.play: Have batter'd me like roaring cannon-shot,

81.play: And made me almost yield upon my knees.

82.play: Forgive me, country, and sweet countrymen,

83.play: And, lords, accept this hearty kind embrace:

84.play: My forces and my power of men are yours:

85.play: So farewell, Talbot; I'll no longer trust thee.

86.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: [Aside]  Done like a Frenchman: turn, and turn again!

87.play: CHARLES: Welcome, brave duke! thy friendship makes us fresh.

88.play: BASTARD OF ORLEANS: And doth beget new courage in our breasts.

89.play: ALENCON: Pucelle hath bravely play'd her part in this,

90.play: And doth deserve a coronet of gold.

91.play: CHARLES: Now let us on, my lords, and join our powers,

92.play: And seek how we may prejudice the foe.

1.play: [Enter KING HENRY VI, GLOUCESTER, BISHOP OF  WINCHESTER, YORK, SUFFOLK, SOMERSET, WARWICK,  EXETER, VERNON	BASSET, and others. To them with his Soldiers, TALBOT]

2.play: TALBOT: My gracious prince, and honourable peers,

3.play: Hearing of your arrival in this realm,

4.play: I have awhile given truce unto my wars,

5.play: To do my duty to my sovereign:

6.play: In sign, whereof, this arm, that hath reclaim'd

7.play: To your obedience fifty fortresses,

8.play: Twelve cities and seven walled towns of strength,

9.play: Beside five hundred prisoners of esteem,

10.play: Lets fall his sword before your highness' feet,

11.play: And with submissive loyalty of heart

12.play: Ascribes the glory of his conquest got

13.play: First to my God and next unto your grace.

14.play: KING HENRY VI: Is this the Lord Talbot, uncle Gloucester,

15.play: That hath so long been resident in France?

16.play: GLOUCESTER: Yes, if it please your majesty, my liege.

17.play: KING HENRY VI: Welcome, brave captain and victorious lord!

18.play: When I was young, as yet I am not old,

19.play: I do remember how my father said

20.play: A stouter champion never handled sword.

21.play: Long since we were resolved of your truth,

22.play: Your faithful service and your toil in war;

23.play: Yet never have you tasted our reward,

24.play: Or been reguerdon'd with so much as thanks,

25.play: Because till now we never saw your face:

26.play: Therefore, stand up; and, for these good deserts,

27.play: We here create you Earl of Shrewsbury;

28.play: And in our coronation take your place.

29.play: VERNON: Now, sir, to you, that were so hot at sea,

30.play: Disgracing of these colours that I wear

31.play: In honour of my noble Lord of York:

32.play: Darest thou maintain the former words thou spakest?

33.play: BASSET: Yes, sir; as well as you dare patronage

34.play: The envious barking of your saucy tongue

35.play: Against my lord the Duke of Somerset.

36.play: VERNON: Sirrah, thy lord I honour as he is.

37.play: BASSET: Why, what is he? as good a man as York.

38.play: VERNON: Hark ye; not so: in witness, take ye that.

39.play: BASSET: Villain, thou know'st the law of arms is such

40.play: That whoso draws a sword, 'tis present death,

41.play: Or else this blow should broach thy dearest blood.

42.play: But I'll unto his majesty, and crave

43.play: I may have liberty to venge this wrong;

44.play: When thou shalt see I'll meet thee to thy cost.

45.play: VERNON: Well, miscreant, I'll be there as soon as you;

46.play: And, after, meet you sooner than you would.
