1.play: [Enter KING HENRY VI, GLOUCESTER, BISHOP OF  WINCHESTER, YORK, SUFFOLK, SOMERSET, WARWICK, TALBOT, EXETER, the Governor, of Paris, and others]

2.play: GLOUCESTER: Lord bishop, set the crown upon his head.

3.play: BISHOP

4.play: OF WINCHESTER: God save King Henry, of that name the sixth!

5.play: GLOUCESTER: Now, governor of Paris, take your oath,

6.play: That you elect no other king but him;

7.play: Esteem none friends but such as are his friends,

8.play: And none your foes but such as shall pretend

9.play: Malicious practises against his state:

10.play: This shall ye do, so help you righteous God!

11.play: FASTOLFE: My gracious sovereign, as I rode from Calais,

12.play: To haste unto your coronation,

13.play: A letter was deliver'd to my hands,

14.play: Writ to your grace from the Duke of Burgundy.

15.play: TALBOT: Shame to the Duke of Burgundy and thee!

16.play: I vow'd, base knight, when I did meet thee next,

17.play: To tear the garter from thy craven's leg,

18.play: Which I have done, because unworthily

19.play: Thou wast installed in that high degree.

20.play: Pardon me, princely Henry, and the rest

21.play: This dastard, at the battle of Patay,

22.play: When but in all I was six thousand strong

23.play: And that the French were almost ten to one,

24.play: Before we met or that a stroke was given,

25.play: Like to a trusty squire did run away:

26.play: In which assault we lost twelve hundred men;

27.play: Myself and divers gentlemen beside

28.play: Were there surprised and taken prisoners.

29.play: Then judge, great lords, if I have done amiss;

30.play: Or whether that such cowards ought to wear

31.play: This ornament of knighthood, yea or no.

32.play: GLOUCESTER: To say the truth, this fact was infamous

33.play: And ill beseeming any common man,

34.play: Much more a knight, a captain and a leader.

35.play: TALBOT: When first this order was ordain'd, my lords,

36.play: Knights of the garter were of noble birth,

37.play: Valiant and virtuous, full of haughty courage,

38.play: Such as were grown to credit by the wars;

39.play: Not fearing death, nor shrinking for distress,

40.play: But always resolute in most extremes.

41.play: He then that is not furnish'd in this sort

42.play: Doth but usurp the sacred name of knight,

43.play: Profaning this most honourable order,

44.play: And should, if I were worthy to be judge,

45.play: Be quite degraded, like a hedge-born swain

46.play: That doth presume to boast of gentle blood.

47.play: KING HENRY VI: Stain to thy countrymen, thou hear'st thy doom!

48.play: Be packing, therefore, thou that wast a knight:

49.play: Henceforth we banish thee, on pain of death.

50.play: And now, my lord protector, view the letter

51.play: Sent from our uncle Duke of Burgundy.

52.play: GLOUCESTER: What means his grace, that he hath changed his style?

53.play: No more but, plain and bluntly, 'To the king!'

54.play: Hath he forgot he is his sovereign?

56.play: Pretend some alteration in good will?

59.play: Moved with compassion of my country's wreck,

61.play: Of such as your oppression feeds upon,

63.play: And join'd with Charles, the rightful King of France.'

64.play: O monstrous treachery! can this be so,

65.play: That in alliance, amity and oaths,

66.play: There should be found such false dissembling guile?

67.play: KING HENRY VI: What! doth my uncle Burgundy revolt?

68.play: GLOUCESTER: He doth, my lord, and is become your foe.

69.play: KING HENRY VI: Is that the worst this letter doth contain?

70.play: GLOUCESTER: It is the worst, and all, my lord, he writes.

71.play: KING HENRY VI: Why, then, Lord Talbot there shall talk with him

72.play: And give him chastisement for this abuse.

73.play: How say you, my lord? are you not content?

74.play: TALBOT: Content, my liege! yes, but that I am prevented,

75.play: I should have begg'd I might have been employ'd.

76.play: KING HENRY VI: Then gather strength and march unto him straight:

77.play: Let him perceive how ill we brook his treason

78.play: And what offence it is to flout his friends.

79.play: TALBOT: I go, my lord, in heart desiring still

80.play: You may behold confusion of your foes.

81.play: VERNON: Grant me the combat, gracious sovereign.

82.play: BASSET: And me, my lord, grant me the combat too.

83.play: YORK: This is my servant: hear him, noble prince.

84.play: SOMERSET: And this is mine: sweet Henry, favour him.

85.play: KING HENRY VI: Be patient, lords; and give them leave to speak.

86.play: Say, gentlemen, what makes you thus exclaim?

87.play: And wherefore crave you combat? or with whom?

88.play: VERNON: With him, my lord; for he hath done me wrong.

89.play: BASSET: And I with him; for he hath done me wrong.

90.play: KING HENRY VI: What is that wrong whereof you both complain?

91.play: First let me know, and then I'll answer you.

92.play: BASSET: Crossing the sea from England into France,

93.play: This fellow here, with envious carping tongue,

94.play: Upbraided me about the rose I wear;

95.play: Saying, the sanguine colour of the leaves

96.play: Did represent my master's blushing cheeks,

97.play: When stubbornly he did repugn the truth

98.play: About a certain question in the law

99.play: Argued betwixt the Duke of York and him;

100.play: With other vile and ignominious terms:

101.play: In confutation of which rude reproach

102.play: And in defence of my lord's worthiness,

103.play: I crave the benefit of law of arms.

104.play: VERNON: And that is my petition, noble lord:

105.play: For though he seem with forged quaint conceit

106.play: To set a gloss upon his bold intent,

107.play: Yet know, my lord, I was provoked by him;

108.play: And he first took exceptions at this badge,

109.play: Pronouncing that the paleness of this flower

110.play: Bewray'd the faintness of my master's heart.

111.play: YORK: Will not this malice, Somerset, be left?

112.play: SOMERSET: Your private grudge, my Lord of York, will out,

113.play: Though ne'er so cunningly you smother it.

114.play: KING HENRY VI: Good Lord, what madness rules in brainsick men,

115.play: When for so slight and frivolous a cause

117.play: Good cousins both, of York and Somerset,

118.play: Quiet yourselves, I pray, and be at peace.

119.play: YORK: Let this dissension first be tried by fight,

120.play: And then your highness shall command a peace.

121.play: SOMERSET: The quarrel toucheth none but us alone;

122.play: Betwixt ourselves let us decide it then.

123.play: YORK: There is my pledge; accept it, Somerset.

124.play: VERNON: Nay, let it rest where it began at first.

125.play: BASSET: Confirm it so, mine honourable lord.

126.play: GLOUCESTER: Confirm it so! Confounded be your strife!

127.play: And perish ye, with your audacious prate!

128.play: Presumptuous vassals, are you not ashamed

130.play: To trouble and disturb the king and us?

131.play: And you, my lords, methinks you do not well

132.play: To bear with their perverse objections;

133.play: Much less to take occasion from their mouths

134.play: To raise a mutiny betwixt yourselves:

135.play: Let me persuade you take a better course.

136.play: EXETER: It grieves his highness: good my lords, be friends.

137.play: KING HENRY VI: Come hither, you that would be combatants:

138.play: Henceforth I charge you, as you love our favour,

139.play: Quite to forget this quarrel and the cause.

140.play: And you, my lords, remember where we are,

141.play: In France, amongst a fickle wavering nation:

142.play: If they perceive dissension in our looks

143.play: And that within ourselves we disagree,

144.play: How will their grudging stomachs be provoked

146.play: Beside, what infamy will there arise,

147.play: When foreign princes shall be certified

148.play: That for a toy, a thing of no regard,

149.play: King Henry's peers and chief nobility

150.play: Destroy'd themselves, and lost the realm of France!

151.play: O, think upon the conquest of my father,

152.play: My tender years, and let us not forego

153.play: That for a trifle that was bought with blood

154.play: Let me be umpire in this doubtful strife.

155.play: I see no reason, if I wear this rose,

156.play: That any one should therefore be suspicious

157.play: I more incline to Somerset than York:

158.play: Both are my kinsmen, and I love them both:

159.play: As well they may upbraid me with my crown,

160.play: Because, forsooth, the king of Scots is crown'd.

161.play: But your discretions better can persuade

162.play: Than I am able to instruct or teach:

163.play: And therefore, as we hither came in peace,

164.play: So let us still continue peace and love.

165.play: Cousin of York, we institute your grace

166.play: To be our regent in these parts of France:

167.play: And, good my Lord of Somerset, unite

168.play: Your troops of horsemen with his bands of foot;

169.play: And, like true subjects, sons of your progenitors,

171.play: Your angry choler on your enemies.

172.play: Ourself, my lord protector and the rest

173.play: After some respite will return to Calais;

174.play: From thence to England; where I hope ere long

175.play: To be presented, by your victories,

176.play: With Charles, Alencon and that traitorous rout.

177.play: WARWICK: My Lord of York, I promise you, the king

178.play: Prettily, methought, did play the orator.

179.play: YORK: And so he did; but yet I like it not,

180.play: In that he wears the badge of Somerset.

181.play: WARWICK: Tush, that was but his fancy, blame him not;

182.play: I dare presume, sweet prince, he thought no harm.

183.play: YORK: An if I wist he did,--but let it rest;

184.play: Other affairs must now be managed.

185.play: EXETER: Well didst thou, Richard, to suppress thy voice;

186.play: For, had the passions of thy heart burst out,

187.play: I fear we should have seen decipher'd there

188.play: More rancorous spite, more furious raging broils,

189.play: Than yet can be imagined or supposed.

190.play: But howsoe'er, no simple man that sees

192.play: This shouldering of each other in the court,

193.play: This factious bandying of their favourites,

194.play: But that it doth presage some ill event.

195.play: 'Tis much when sceptres are in children's hands;

196.play: But more when envy breeds unkind division;

197.play: There comes the rain, there begins confusion.

1.play: [Enter TALBOT, with trump and drum]

2.play: TALBOT: Go to the gates of Bourdeaux, trumpeter:

3.play: Summon their general unto the wall.

4.play: English John Talbot, captains, calls you forth,

5.play: Servant in arms to Harry King of England;

6.play: And thus he would: Open your city gates;

7.play: Be humble to us; call my sovereign yours,

8.play: And do him homage as obedient subjects;

9.play: And I'll withdraw me and my bloody power:

10.play: But, if you frown upon this proffer'd peace,

11.play: You tempt the fury of my three attendants,

12.play: Lean famine, quartering steel, and climbing fire;

13.play: Who in a moment even with the earth

14.play: Shall lay your stately and air-braving towers,

15.play: If you forsake the offer of their love.

16.play: He fables not; I hear the enemy:

17.play: Out, some light horsemen, and peruse their wings.

18.play: O, negligent and heedless discipline!

19.play: How are we park'd and bounded in a pale,

20.play: A little herd of England's timorous deer,

21.play: Mazed with a yelping kennel of French curs!

22.play: If we be English deer, be then in blood;

23.play: Not rascal-like, to fall down with a pinch,

24.play: But rather, moody-mad and desperate stags,

25.play: Turn on the bloody hounds with heads of steel

26.play: And make the cowards stand aloof at bay:

27.play: Sell every man his life as dear as mine,

28.play: And they shall find dear deer of us, my friends.

29.play: God and Saint George, Talbot and England's right,

30.play: Prosper our colours in this dangerous fight!

1.play: [Enter a Messenger that meets YORK. Enter YORK with trumpet and many Soldiers]

2.play: YORK: Are not the speedy scouts return'd again,

3.play: That dogg'd the mighty army of the Dauphin?

4.play: A plague upon that villain Somerset,

5.play: That thus delays my promised supply

6.play: Of horsemen, that were levied for this siege!

7.play: Renowned Talbot doth expect my aid,

8.play: And I am lowted by a traitor villain

9.play: And cannot help the noble chevalier:

10.play: God comfort him in this necessity!

11.play: If he miscarry, farewell wars in France.

12.play: LUCY: Thou princely leader of our English strength,

13.play: Never so needful on the earth of France,

14.play: Spur to the rescue of the noble Talbot,

15.play: Who now is girdled with a waist of iron

16.play: And hemm'd about with grim destruction:

17.play: To Bourdeaux, warlike duke! to Bourdeaux, York!

18.play: Else, farewell Talbot, France, and England's honour.

19.play: YORK: O God, that Somerset, who in proud heart

20.play: Doth stop my cornets, were in Talbot's place!

21.play: So should we save a valiant gentleman

22.play: By forfeiting a traitor and a coward.

23.play: Mad ire and wrathful fury makes me weep,

24.play: That thus we die, while remiss traitors sleep.

25.play: LUCY: O, send some succor to the distress'd lord!

26.play: YORK: He dies, we lose; I break my warlike word;

27.play: We mourn, France smiles; we lose, they daily get;

28.play: All 'long of this vile traitor Somerset.

29.play: LUCY: Then God take mercy on brave Talbot's soul;

30.play: And on his son young John, who two hours since

31.play: I met in travel toward his warlike father!

32.play: This seven years did not Talbot see his son;

33.play: And now they meet where both their lives are done.

34.play: YORK: Alas, what joy shall noble Talbot have

35.play: To bid his young son welcome to his grave?

36.play: Away! vexation almost stops my breath,

37.play: That sunder'd friends greet in the hour of death.

38.play: Lucy, farewell; no more my fortune can,

39.play: But curse the cause I cannot aid the man.

40.play: Maine, Blois, Poictiers, and Tours, are won away,

41.play: 'Long all of Somerset and his delay.

42.play: LUCY: Thus, while the vulture of sedition

43.play: Feeds in the bosom of such great commanders,

44.play: Sleeping neglection doth betray to loss

45.play: The conquest of our scarce cold conqueror,

46.play: That ever living man of memory,

47.play: Henry the Fifth: whiles they each other cross,

48.play: Lives, honours, lands and all hurry to loss.

1.play: [Enter SOMERSET, with his army; a Captain of TALBOT's with him]

2.play: SOMERSET: It is too late; I cannot send them now:

3.play: This expedition was by York and Talbot

4.play: Too rashly plotted: all our general force

5.play: Might with a sally of the very town

6.play: Be buckled with: the over-daring Talbot

7.play: Hath sullied all his gloss of former honour

8.play: By this unheedful, desperate, wild adventure:

9.play: York set him on to fight and die in shame,

10.play: That, Talbot dead, great York might bear the name.

11.play: [Enter Sir William LUCY]

12.play: SOMERSET: How now, Sir William! whither were you sent?

13.play: LUCY: Whither, my lord? from bought and sold Lord Talbot;

14.play: Who, ring'd about with bold adversity,

15.play: Cries out for noble York and Somerset,

16.play: To beat assailing death from his weak legions:

17.play: And whiles the honourable captain there

18.play: Drops bloody sweat from his war-wearied limbs,

19.play: And, in advantage lingering, looks for rescue,

20.play: You, his false hopes, the trust of England's honour,

21.play: Keep off aloof with worthless emulation.

22.play: Let not your private discord keep away

23.play: The levied succors that should lend him aid,

24.play: While he, renowned noble gentleman,

25.play: Yields up his life unto a world of odds:

26.play: Orleans the Bastard, Charles, Burgundy,

27.play: Alencon, Reignier, compass him about,

28.play: And Talbot perisheth by your default.

29.play: SOMERSET: York set him on; York should have sent him aid.

30.play: LUCY: And York as fast upon your grace exclaims;

31.play: Swearing that you withhold his levied host,

32.play: Collected for this expedition.

33.play: SOMERSET: York lies; he might have sent and had the horse;

34.play: I owe him little duty, and less love;

35.play: And take foul scorn to fawn on him by sending.

36.play: LUCY: The fraud of England, not the force of France,

37.play: Hath now entrapp'd the noble-minded Talbot:

38.play: Never to England shall he bear his life;

39.play: But dies, betray'd to fortune by your strife.

40.play: SOMERSET: Come, go; I will dispatch the horsemen straight:

41.play: Within six hours they will be at his aid.

42.play: LUCY: Too late comes rescue: he is ta'en or slain;

43.play: For fly he could not, if he would have fled;

44.play: And fly would Talbot never, though he might.

45.play: SOMERSET: If he be dead, brave Talbot, then adieu!

46.play: LUCY: His fame lives in the world, his shame in you.

1.play: [Enter TALBOT and JOHN his son]

2.play: TALBOT: O young John Talbot! I did send for thee

3.play: To tutor thee in stratagems of war,

4.play: That Talbot's name might be in thee revived

5.play: When sapless age and weak unable limbs

6.play: Should bring thy father to his drooping chair.

7.play: But, O malignant and ill-boding stars!

8.play: Now thou art come unto a feast of death,

9.play: A terrible and unavoided danger:

10.play: Therefore, dear boy, mount on my swiftest horse;

11.play: And I'll direct thee how thou shalt escape

12.play: By sudden flight: come, dally not, be gone.

13.play: JOHN TALBOT: Is my name Talbot? and am I your son?

14.play: And shall I fly? O if you love my mother,

15.play: Dishonour not her honourable name,

16.play: To make a bastard and a slave of me!

17.play: The world will say, he is not Talbot's blood,

18.play: That basely fled when noble Talbot stood.

19.play: TALBOT: Fly, to revenge my death, if I be slain.

20.play: JOHN TALBOT: He that flies so will ne'er return again.

21.play: TALBOT: If we both stay, we both are sure to die.

22.play: JOHN TALBOT: Then let me stay; and, father, do you fly:

23.play: Your loss is great, so your regard should be;

24.play: My worth unknown, no loss is known in me.

25.play: Upon my death the French can little boast;

26.play: In yours they will, in you all hopes are lost.

27.play: Flight cannot stain the honour you have won;

28.play: But mine it will, that no exploit have done:

29.play: You fled for vantage, everyone will swear;

30.play: But, if I bow, they'll say it was for fear.

31.play: There is no hope that ever I will stay,

32.play: If the first hour I shrink and run away.

33.play: Here on my knee I beg mortality,

34.play: Rather than life preserved with infamy.

35.play: TALBOT: Shall all thy mother's hopes lie in one tomb?

36.play: JOHN TALBOT: Ay, rather than I'll shame my mother's womb.

37.play: TALBOT: Upon my blessing, I command thee go.

38.play: JOHN TALBOT: To fight I will, but not to fly the foe.

39.play: TALBOT: Part of thy father may be saved in thee.

40.play: JOHN TALBOT: No part of him but will be shame in me.

41.play: TALBOT: Thou never hadst renown, nor canst not lose it.

42.play: JOHN TALBOT: Yes, your renowned name: shall flight abuse it?

43.play: TALBOT: Thy father's charge shall clear thee from that stain.

44.play: JOHN TALBOT: You cannot witness for me, being slain.

45.play: If death be so apparent, then both fly.

46.play: TALBOT: And leave my followers here to fight and die?

47.play: My age was never tainted with such shame.

48.play: JOHN TALBOT: And shall my youth be guilty of such blame?

49.play: No more can I be sever'd from your side,

50.play: Than can yourself yourself in twain divide:

51.play: Stay, go, do what you will, the like do I;

52.play: For live I will not, if my father die.

53.play: TALBOT: Then here I take my leave of thee, fair son,

54.play: Born to eclipse thy life this afternoon.

55.play: Come, side by side together live and die.

56.play: And soul with soul from France to heaven fly.

1.play: [Alarum: excursions, wherein JOHN TALBOT is hemmed about, and TALBOT rescues him]

2.play: TALBOT: Saint George and victory! fight, soldiers, fight.

3.play: The regent hath with Talbot broke his word

4.play: And left us to the rage of France his sword.

5.play: Where is John Talbot? Pause, and take thy breath;

6.play: I gave thee life and rescued thee from death.

7.play: JOHN TALBOT: O, twice my father, twice am I thy son!

8.play: The life thou gavest me first was lost and done,

9.play: Till with thy warlike sword, despite of late,

10.play: To my determined time thou gavest new date.

11.play: TALBOT: When from the Dauphin's crest thy sword struck fire,

12.play: It warm'd thy father's heart with proud desire

13.play: Of bold-faced victory. Then leaden age,

14.play: Quicken'd with youthful spleen and warlike rage,

15.play: Beat down Alencon, Orleans, Burgundy,

16.play: And from the pride of Gallia rescued thee.

17.play: The ireful bastard Orleans, that drew blood

18.play: From thee, my boy, and had the maidenhood

19.play: Of thy first fight, I soon encountered,

20.play: And interchanging blows I quickly shed

21.play: Some of his bastard blood; and in disgrace

22.play: Bespoke him thus; 'Contaminated, base

23.play: And misbegotten blood I spill of thine,

24.play: Mean and right poor, for that pure blood of mine

25.play: Which thou didst force from Talbot, my brave boy:'

26.play: Here, purposing the Bastard to destroy,

27.play: Came in strong rescue. Speak, thy father's care,

28.play: Art thou not weary, John? how dost thou fare?

29.play: Wilt thou yet leave the battle, boy, and fly,

30.play: Now thou art seal'd the son of chivalry?

31.play: Fly, to revenge my death when I am dead:

32.play: The help of one stands me in little stead.

33.play: O, too much folly is it, well I wot,

34.play: To hazard all our lives in one small boat!

35.play: If I to-day die not with Frenchmen's rage,

36.play: To-morrow I shall die with mickle age:

37.play: By me they nothing gain an if I stay;

38.play: 'Tis but the shortening of my life one day:

39.play: In thee thy mother dies, our household's name,

40.play: My death's revenge, thy youth, and England's fame:

41.play: All these and more we hazard by thy stay;

42.play: All these are saved if thou wilt fly away.

43.play: JOHN TALBOT: The sword of Orleans hath not made me smart;

44.play: These words of yours draw life-blood from my heart:

45.play: On that advantage, bought with such a shame,

46.play: To save a paltry life and slay bright fame,

47.play: Before young Talbot from old Talbot fly,

48.play: The coward horse that bears me fail and die!

49.play: And like me to the peasant boys of France,

50.play: To be shame's scorn and subject of mischance!

51.play: Surely, by all the glory you have won,

52.play: An if I fly, I am not Talbot's son:

53.play: Then talk no more of flight, it is no boot;

54.play: If son to Talbot, die at Talbot's foot.

55.play: TALBOT: Then follow thou thy desperate sire of Crete,

56.play: Thou Icarus; thy life to me is sweet:

57.play: If thou wilt fight, fight by thy father's side;

58.play: And, commendable proved, let's die in pride.

1.play: [Alarum: excursions. Enter TALBOT led by a Servant]

2.play: TALBOT: Where is my other life? mine own is gone;

3.play: O, where's young Talbot? where is valiant John?

4.play: Triumphant death, smear'd with captivity,

5.play: Young Talbot's valour makes me smile at thee:

6.play: When he perceived me shrink and on my knee,

7.play: His bloody sword he brandish'd over me,

8.play: And, like a hungry lion, did commence

9.play: Rough deeds of rage and stern impatience;

10.play: But when my angry guardant stood alone,

11.play: Tendering my ruin and assail'd of none,

12.play: Dizzy-eyed fury and great rage of heart

13.play: Suddenly made him from my side to start

14.play: Into the clustering battle of the French;

15.play: And in that sea of blood my boy did drench

16.play: His over-mounting spirit, and there died,

17.play: My Icarus, my blossom, in his pride.

18.play: [Enter Soldiers, with the body of JOHN TALBOT]

19.play: TALBOT: Thou antic death, which laugh'st us here to scorn,

20.play: Anon, from thy insulting tyranny,

21.play: Coupled in bonds of perpetuity,

22.play: Two Talbots, winged through the lither sky,

23.play: In thy despite shall 'scape mortality.

24.play: O, thou, whose wounds become hard-favour'd death,

25.play: Speak to thy father ere thou yield thy breath!

26.play: Brave death by speaking, whether he will or no;

27.play: Imagine him a Frenchman and thy foe.

28.play: Poor boy! he smiles, methinks, as who should say,

29.play: Had death been French, then death had died to-day.

30.play: Come, come and lay him in his father's arms:

31.play: My spirit can no longer bear these harms.

32.play: Soldiers, adieu! I have what I would have,

33.play: Now my old arms are young John Talbot's grave.

34.play: CHARLES: Had York and Somerset brought rescue in,

35.play: We should have found a bloody day of this.

36.play: BASTARD OF ORLEANS: How the young whelp of Talbot's, raging-wood,

37.play: Did flesh his puny sword in Frenchmen's blood!

38.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: Once I encounter'd him, and thus I said:

39.play: 'Thou maiden youth, be vanquish'd by a maid:'

40.play: But, with a proud majestical high scorn,

41.play: He answer'd thus: 'Young Talbot was not born

42.play: To be the pillage of a giglot wench:'

43.play: So, rushing in the bowels of the French,

44.play: He left me proudly, as unworthy fight.

45.play: BURGUNDY: Doubtless he would have made a noble knight;

46.play: See, where he lies inhearsed in the arms

47.play: Of the most bloody nurser of his harms!

48.play: BASTARD OF ORLEANS: Hew them to pieces, hack their bones asunder

49.play: Whose life was England's glory, Gallia's wonder.

50.play: CHARLES: O, no, forbear! for that which we have fled

51.play: During the life, let us not wrong it dead.

52.play: LUCY: Herald, conduct me to the Dauphin's tent,

53.play: To know who hath obtained the glory of the day.

54.play: CHARLES: On what submissive message art thou sent?

55.play: LUCY: Submission, Dauphin! 'tis a mere French word;

56.play: We English warriors wot not what it means.

57.play: I come to know what prisoners thou hast ta'en

58.play: And to survey the bodies of the dead.

59.play: CHARLES: For prisoners ask'st thou? hell our prison is.

60.play: But tell me whom thou seek'st.

61.play: LUCY: But where's the great Alcides of the field,

62.play: Valiant Lord Talbot, Earl of Shrewsbury,

63.play: Created, for his rare success in arms,

64.play: Great Earl of Washford, Waterford and Valence;

65.play: Lord Talbot of Goodrig and Urchinfield,

66.play: Lord Strange of Blackmere, Lord Verdun of Alton,

67.play: Lord Cromwell of Wingfield, Lord Furnival of Sheffield,

69.play: Knight of the noble order of Saint George,

70.play: Worthy Saint Michael and the Golden Fleece;

71.play: Great marshal to Henry the Sixth

72.play: Of all his wars within the realm of France?

73.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: Here is a silly stately style indeed!

74.play: The Turk, that two and fifty kingdoms hath,

75.play: Writes not so tedious a style as this.

76.play: Him that thou magnifiest with all these titles

77.play: Stinking and fly-blown lies here at our feet.

78.play: LUCY: Is Talbot slain, the Frenchmen's only scourge,

79.play: Your kingdom's terror and black Nemesis?

80.play: O, were mine eyeballs into bullets turn'd,

81.play: That I in rage might shoot them at your faces!

82.play: O, that I could but call these dead to life!

83.play: It were enough to fright the realm of France:

84.play: Were but his picture left amongst you here,

85.play: It would amaze the proudest of you all.

86.play: Give me their bodies, that I may bear them hence

87.play: And give them burial as beseems their worth.

88.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: I think this upstart is old Talbot's ghost,

89.play: [He speaks with such a proud commanding spirit.]

90.play: For God's sake let him have 'em; to keep them here,

91.play: They would but stink, and putrefy the air.

92.play: CHARLES: Go, take their bodies hence.

93.play: LUCY: I'll bear them hence; but from their ashes shall be rear'd

94.play: A phoenix that shall make all France afeard.

95.play: CHARLES: So we be rid of them, do with 'em what thou wilt.

96.play: And now to Paris, in this conquering vein:

97.play: All will be ours, now bloody Talbot's slain.

1.play: KING HENRY VI: Have you perused the letters from the pope,

2.play: The emperor and the Earl of Armagnac?

3.play: GLOUCESTER: I have, my lord: and their intent is this:

4.play: They humbly sue unto your excellence

5.play: To have a godly peace concluded of

6.play: Between the realms of England and of France.

7.play: KING HENRY VI: How doth your grace affect their motion?

8.play: GLOUCESTER: Well, my good lord; and as the only means

9.play: To stop effusion of our Christian blood

10.play: And 'stablish quietness on every side.

11.play: KING HENRY VI: Ay, marry, uncle; for I always thought

12.play: It was both impious and unnatural

13.play: That such immanity and bloody strife

14.play: Should reign among professors of one faith.

15.play: GLOUCESTER: Beside, my lord, the sooner to effect

16.play: And surer bind this knot of amity,

17.play: The Earl of Armagnac, near knit to Charles,

18.play: A man of great authority in France,

19.play: Proffers his only daughter to your grace

20.play: In marriage, with a large and sumptuous dowry.

21.play: KING HENRY VI: Marriage, uncle! alas, my years are young!

22.play: And fitter is my study and my books

23.play: Than wanton dalliance with a paramour.

24.play: Yet call the ambassador; and, as you please,

25.play: So let them have their answers every one:

26.play: I shall be well content with any choice

27.play: Tends to God's glory and my country's weal.

28.play: EXETER: What! is my Lord of Winchester install'd,

29.play: And call'd unto a cardinal's degree?

30.play: Then I perceive that will be verified

31.play: Henry the Fifth did sometime prophesy,

32.play: 'If once he come to be a cardinal,

33.play: He'll make his cap co-equal with the crown.'

34.play: KING HENRY VI: My lords ambassadors, your several suits

35.play: Have been consider'd and debated on.

36.play: And therefore are we certainly resolved

37.play: To draw conditions of a friendly peace;

38.play: Which by my Lord of Winchester we mean

39.play: Shall be transported presently to France.

40.play: GLOUCESTER: And for the proffer of my lord your master,

41.play: I have inform'd his highness so at large

42.play: As liking of the lady's virtuous gifts,

43.play: Her beauty and the value of her dower,

44.play: He doth intend she shall be England's queen.

45.play: KING HENRY VI: In argument and proof of which contract,

46.play: Bear her this jewel, pledge of my affection.

47.play: And so, my lord protector, see them guarded

48.play: And safely brought to Dover; where inshipp'd

49.play: Commit them to the fortune of the sea.

50.play: CARDINAL

51.play: OF WINCHESTER: Stay, my lord legate: you shall first receive

52.play: The sum of money which I promised

53.play: Should be deliver'd to his holiness

54.play: For clothing me in these grave ornaments.

55.play: [Aside Now Winchester will not submit, I trow, Or be inferior to the proudest peer.]

57.play: Humphrey of Gloucester, thou shalt well perceive

58.play: That, neither in birth or for authority,

59.play: The bishop will be overborne by thee:

60.play: I'll either make thee stoop and bend thy knee,

61.play: Or sack this country with a mutiny.

1.play: [Enter CHARLES, BURGUNDY, ALENCON, BASTARD OF ORLEANS, REIGNIER, JOAN LA PUCELLE, and forces]

2.play: CHARLES: These news, my lord, may cheer our drooping spirits:

3.play: 'Tis said the stout Parisians do revolt

4.play: And turn again unto the warlike French.

5.play: ALENCON: Then march to Paris, royal Charles of France,

6.play: And keep not back your powers in dalliance.

7.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: Peace be amongst them, if they turn to us;

8.play: Else, ruin combat with their palaces!

9.play: CHARLES: What tidings send our scouts? I prithee, speak.

10.play: Somewhat too sudden, sirs, the warning is;

11.play: But we will presently provide for them.

12.play: BURGUNDY: I trust the ghost of Talbot is not there:

13.play: Now he is gone, my lord, you need not fear.

14.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: Of all base passions, fear is most accursed.

15.play: Command the conquest, Charles, it shall be thine,

16.play: Let Henry fret and all the world repine.

17.play: CHARLES: Then on, my lords; and France be fortunate!

1.play: [Alarum. Excursions. Enter JOAN LA PUCELLE]

2.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: The regent conquers, and the Frenchmen fly.

3.play: Now help, ye charming spells and periapts;

4.play: And ye choice spirits that admonish me

5.play: And give me signs of future accidents.

6.play: You speedy helpers, that are substitutes

7.play: Under the lordly monarch of the north,

8.play: Appear and aid me in this enterprise.

9.play: This speedy and quick appearance argues proof

10.play: Of your accustom'd diligence to me.

11.play: Now, ye familiar spirits, that are cull'd

12.play: Out of the powerful regions under earth,

13.play: Help me this once, that France may get the field.

14.play: O, hold me not with silence over-long!

15.play: Where I was wont to feed you with my blood,

16.play: I'll lop a member off and give it you

17.play: In earnest of further benefit,

18.play: So you do condescend to help me now.

19.play: No hope to have redress? My body shall

20.play: Pay recompense, if you will grant my suit.

21.play: Cannot my body nor blood-sacrifice

22.play: Entreat you to your wonted furtherance?

23.play: Then take my soul, my body, soul and all,

24.play: Before that England give the French the foil.

25.play: See, they forsake me! Now the time is come

26.play: That France must vail her lofty-plumed crest

27.play: And let her head fall into England's lap.

28.play: My ancient incantations are too weak,

29.play: And hell too strong for me to buckle with:

30.play: Now, France, thy glory droopeth to the dust.

31.play: YORK: Damsel of France, I think I have you fast:

32.play: Unchain your spirits now with spelling charms

33.play: And try if they can gain your liberty.

34.play: A goodly prize, fit for the devil's grace!

35.play: See, how the ugly wench doth bend her brows,

36.play: As if with Circe she would change my shape!

37.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: Changed to a worser shape thou canst not be.

38.play: YORK: O, Charles the Dauphin is a proper man;

39.play: No shape but his can please your dainty eye.

40.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: A plaguing mischief light on Charles and thee!

41.play: And may ye both be suddenly surprised

42.play: By bloody hands, in sleeping on your beds!

43.play: YORK: Fell banning hag, enchantress, hold thy tongue!

44.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: I prithee, give me leave to curse awhile.

45.play: YORK: Curse, miscreant, when thou comest to the stake.

46.play: SUFFOLK: Be what thou wilt, thou art my prisoner.

47.play: O fairest beauty, do not fear nor fly!

48.play: For I will touch thee but with reverent hands;

49.play: I kiss these fingers for eternal peace,

50.play: And lay them gently on thy tender side.

51.play: Who art thou? say, that I may honour thee.

52.play: MARGARET: Margaret my name, and daughter to a king,

53.play: The King of Naples, whosoe'er thou art.

54.play: SUFFOLK: An earl I am, and Suffolk am I call'd.

56.play: Thou art allotted to be ta'en by me:

57.play: So doth the swan her downy cygnets save,

58.play: Keeping them prisoner underneath her wings.

59.play: Yet, if this servile usage once offend.

60.play: Go, and be free again, as Suffolk's friend.

61.play: O, stay! I have no power to let her pass;

62.play: My hand would free her, but my heart says no

63.play: As plays the sun upon the glassy streams,

65.play: So seems this gorgeous beauty to mine eyes.

66.play: Fain would I woo her, yet I dare not speak:

67.play: I'll call for pen and ink, and write my mind.

68.play: Fie, de la Pole! disable not thyself;

69.play: Hast not a tongue? is she not here?

70.play: Wilt thou be daunted at a woman's sight?

71.play: Ay, beauty's princely majesty is such,

72.play: Confounds the tongue and makes the senses rough.

73.play: MARGARET: Say, Earl of Suffolk--if thy name be so--

74.play: What ransom must I pay before I pass?

75.play: For I perceive I am thy prisoner.

76.play: SUFFOLK: How canst thou tell she will deny thy suit,

77.play: Before thou make a trial of her love?

78.play: MARGARET: Why speak'st thou not? what ransom must I pay?

79.play: SUFFOLK: She's beautiful, and therefore to be woo'd;

80.play: She is a woman, therefore to be won.

81.play: MARGARET: Wilt thou accept of ransom? yea, or no.

82.play: SUFFOLK: Fond man, remember that thou hast a wife;

83.play: Then how can Margaret be thy paramour?

84.play: MARGARET: I were best to leave him, for he will not hear.

85.play: SUFFOLK: There all is marr'd; there lies a cooling card.

86.play: MARGARET: He talks at random; sure, the man is mad.

87.play: SUFFOLK: And yet a dispensation may be had.

88.play: MARGARET: And yet I would that you would answer me.

89.play: SUFFOLK: I'll win this Lady Margaret. For whom?

90.play: Why, for my king: tush, that's a wooden thing!

91.play: MARGARET: He talks of wood: it is some carpenter.

92.play: SUFFOLK: Yet so my fancy may be satisfied,

93.play: And peace established between these realms

94.play: But there remains a scruple in that too;

95.play: For though her father be the King of Naples,

96.play: Duke of Anjou and Maine, yet is he poor,

97.play: And our nobility will scorn the match.

98.play: MARGARET: Hear ye, captain, are you not at leisure?

99.play: SUFFOLK: It shall be so, disdain they ne'er so much.

100.play: Henry is youthful and will quickly yield.

101.play: Madam, I have a secret to reveal.

102.play: MARGARET: What though I be enthrall'd? he seems a knight,

103.play: And will not any way dishonour me.

104.play: SUFFOLK: Lady, vouchsafe to listen what I say.

105.play: MARGARET: Perhaps I shall be rescued by the French;

106.play: And then I need not crave his courtesy.

107.play: SUFFOLK: Sweet madam, give me a hearing in a cause--

108.play: MARGARET: Tush, women have been captivate ere now.

109.play: SUFFOLK: Lady, wherefore talk you so?

110.play: MARGARET: I cry you mercy, 'tis but Quid for Quo.

111.play: SUFFOLK: Say, gentle princess, would you not suppose

112.play: Your bondage happy, to be made a queen?

113.play: MARGARET: To be a queen in bondage is more vile

114.play: Than is a slave in base servility;

117.play: If happy England's royal king be free.

118.play: MARGARET: Why, what concerns his freedom unto me?

119.play: SUFFOLK: I'll undertake to make thee Henry's queen,

120.play: To put a golden sceptre in thy hand

121.play: And set a precious crown upon thy head,

122.play: If thou wilt condescend to be my--

125.play: MARGARET: I am unworthy to be Henry's wife.

126.play: SUFFOLK: No, gentle madam; I unworthy am

127.play: To woo so fair a dame to be his wife,

128.play: And have no portion in the choice myself.

129.play: How say you, madam, are ye so content?

130.play: MARGARET: An if my father please, I am content.

131.play: SUFFOLK: Then call our captains and our colours forth.

132.play: And, madam, at your father's castle walls

133.play: We'll crave a parley, to confer with him.

134.play: See, Reignier, see, thy daughter prisoner!

138.play: I am a soldier, and unapt to weep,

139.play: Or to exclaim on fortune's fickleness.

140.play: SU FFOLK: Yes, there is remedy enough, my lord:

141.play: Consent, and for thy honour give consent,

142.play: Thy daughter shall be wedded to my king;

143.play: Whom I with pain have woo'd and won thereto;

145.play: Hath gained thy daughter princely liberty.

146.play: REIGNIER: Speaks Suffolk as he thinks?

148.play: That Suffolk doth not flatter, face, or feign.

149.play: REIGNIER: Upon thy princely warrant, I descend

150.play: To give thee answer of thy just demand.

151.play: SUFFOLK: And here I will expect thy coming.

152.play: REIGNIER: Welcome, brave earl, into our territories:

153.play: Command in Anjou what your honour pleases.

154.play: SUFFOLK: Thanks, Reignier, happy for so sweet a child,

155.play: Fit to be made companion with a king:

156.play: What answer makes your grace unto my suit?

157.play: REIGNIER: Since thou dost deign to woo her little worth

158.play: To be the princely bride of such a lord;

160.play: Enjoy mine own, the country Maine and Anjou,

161.play: Free from oppression or the stroke of war,

162.play: My daughter shall be Henry's, if he please.

163.play: SUFFOLK: That is her ransom; I deliver her;

164.play: And those two counties I will undertake

165.play: Your grace shall well and quietly enjoy.

166.play: REIGNIER: And I again, in Henry's royal name,

167.play: As deputy unto that gracious king,

168.play: Give thee her hand, for sign of plighted faith.

169.play: SUFFOLK: Reignier of France, I give thee kingly thanks,

170.play: Because this is in traffic of a king.

171.play: And yet, methinks, I could be well content

172.play: To be mine own attorney in this case.

173.play: I'll over then to England with this news,

174.play: And make this marriage to be solemnized.

175.play: So farewell, Reignier: set this diamond safe

176.play: In golden palaces, as it becomes.

177.play: REIGNIER: I do embrace thee, as I would embrace

178.play: The Christian prince, King Henry, were he here.

179.play: MARGARET: Farewell, my lord: good wishes, praise and prayers

180.play: Shall Suffolk ever have of Margaret.

181.play: SUFFOLK: Farewell, sweet madam: but hark you, Margaret;

182.play: No princely commendations to my king?

183.play: MARGARET: Such commendations as becomes a maid,

184.play: A virgin and his servant, say to him.

185.play: SUFFOLK: Words sweetly placed and modestly directed.

186.play: But madam, I must trouble you again;

187.play: No loving token to his majesty?

188.play: MARGARET: Yes, my good lord, a pure unspotted heart,

189.play: Never yet taint with love, I send the king.

191.play: MARGARET: That for thyself: I will not so presume

192.play: To send such peevish tokens to a king.

193.play: SUFFOLK: O, wert thou for myself! But, Suffolk, stay;

194.play: Thou mayst not wander in that labyrinth;

195.play: There Minotaurs and ugly treasons lurk.

196.play: Solicit Henry with her wondrous praise:

197.play: Bethink thee on her virtues that surmount,

198.play: And natural graces that extinguish art;

199.play: Repeat their semblance often on the seas,

200.play: That, when thou comest to kneel at Henry's feet,

201.play: Thou mayst bereave him of his wits with wonder.

1.play: [Enter YORK, WARWICK, and others]

2.play: YORK: Bring forth that sorceress condemn'd to burn.

3.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: Decrepit miser! base ignoble wretch!

4.play: I am descended of a gentler blood:

5.play: Thou art no father nor no friend of mine.

6.play: WARWICK: Graceless! wilt thou deny thy parentage?

7.play: YORK: This argues what her kind of life hath been,

8.play: Wicked and vile; and so her death concludes.

9.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: Peasant, avaunt! You have suborn'd this man,

10.play: Of purpose to obscure my noble birth.

11.play: [Exit]

12.play: YORK: Take her away; for she hath lived too long,

13.play: To fill the world with vicious qualities.

14.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: First, let me tell you whom you have condemn'd:

15.play: Not me begotten of a shepherd swain,

16.play: But issued from the progeny of kings;

17.play: Virtuous and holy; chosen from above,

18.play: By inspiration of celestial grace,

19.play: To work exceeding miracles on earth.

20.play: I never had to do with wicked spirits:

21.play: But you, that are polluted with your lusts,

22.play: Stain'd with the guiltless blood of innocents,

23.play: Corrupt and tainted with a thousand vices,

24.play: Because you want the grace that others have,

25.play: You judge it straight a thing impossible

26.play: To compass wonders but by help of devils.

27.play: No, misconceived! Joan of Arc hath been

28.play: A virgin from her tender infancy,

29.play: Chaste and immaculate in very thought;

30.play: Whose maiden blood, thus rigorously effused,

31.play: Will cry for vengeance at the gates of heaven.

32.play: YORK: Ay, ay: away with her to execution!

33.play: WARWICK: And hark ye, sirs; because she is a maid,

34.play: Spare for no faggots, let there be enow:

35.play: Place barrels of pitch upon the fatal stake,

36.play: That so her torture may be shortened.

37.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: Will nothing turn your unrelenting hearts?

38.play: Then, Joan, discover thine infirmity,

39.play: That warranteth by law to be thy privilege.

40.play: I am with child, ye bloody homicides:

41.play: Murder not then the fruit within my womb,

42.play: Although ye hale me to a violent death.

43.play: YORK: Now heaven forfend! the holy maid with child!

44.play: WARWICK: The greatest miracle that e'er ye wrought:

45.play: Is all your strict preciseness come to this?

46.play: YORK: She and the Dauphin have been juggling:

47.play: I did imagine what would be her refuge.

48.play: WARWICK: Well, go to; we'll have no bastards live;

49.play: Especially since Charles must father it.

50.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: You are deceived; my child is none of his:

51.play: It was Alencon that enjoy'd my love.

52.play: YORK: Alencon! that notorious Machiavel!

53.play: It dies, an if it had a thousand lives.

54.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: O, give me leave, I have deluded you:

55.play: 'Twas neither Charles nor yet the duke I named,

56.play: But Reignier, king of Naples, that prevail'd.

57.play: WARWICK: A married man! that's most intolerable.

58.play: YORK: Why, here's a girl! I think she knows not well,

59.play: There were so many, whom she may accuse.

60.play: WARWICK: It's sign she hath been liberal and free.

61.play: YORK: And yet, forsooth, she is a virgin pure.

62.play: Strumpet, thy words condemn thy brat and thee:

63.play: Use no entreaty, for it is in vain.

64.play: JOAN LA PUCELLE: Then lead me hence; with whom I leave my curse:

65.play: May never glorious sun reflex his beams

66.play: Upon the country where you make abode;

67.play: But darkness and the gloomy shade of death

68.play: Environ you, till mischief and despair

69.play: Drive you to break your necks or hang yourselves!

70.play: YORK: Break thou in pieces and consume to ashes,

71.play: Thou foul accursed minister of hell!

73.play: OF WINCHESTER: Lord regent, I do greet your excellence

74.play: With letters of commission from the king.

75.play: For know, my lords, the states of Christendom,

76.play: Moved with remorse of these outrageous broils,

77.play: Have earnestly implored a general peace

78.play: Betwixt our nation and the aspiring French;

79.play: And here at hand the Dauphin and his train

80.play: Approacheth, to confer about some matter.

81.play: YORK: Is all our travail turn'd to this effect?

82.play: After the slaughter of so many peers,

83.play: So many captains, gentlemen and soldiers,

84.play: That in this quarrel have been overthrown

85.play: And sold their bodies for their country's benefit,

86.play: Shall we at last conclude effeminate peace?

87.play: Have we not lost most part of all the towns,

88.play: By treason, falsehood and by treachery,

90.play: O Warwick, Warwick! I foresee with grief

91.play: The utter loss of all the realm of France.

92.play: WARWICK: Be patient, York: if we conclude a peace,

93.play: It shall be with such strict and severe covenants

94.play: As little shall the Frenchmen gain thereby.

95.play: CHARLES: Since, lords of England, it is thus agreed

96.play: That peaceful truce shall be proclaim'd in France,

97.play: We come to be informed by yourselves

98.play: What the conditions of that league must be.

99.play: YORK: Speak, Winchester; for boiling choler chokes

100.play: The hollow passage of my poison'd voice,

101.play: By sight of these our baleful enemies.

103.play: OF WINCHESTER: Charles, and the rest, it is enacted thus:

104.play: That, in regard King Henry gives consent,

105.play: Of mere compassion and of lenity,

106.play: To ease your country of distressful war,

107.play: And suffer you to breathe in fruitful peace,

108.play: You shall become true liegemen to his crown:

109.play: And Charles, upon condition thou wilt swear

110.play: To pay him tribute, submit thyself,

111.play: Thou shalt be placed as viceroy under him,

112.play: And still enjoy thy regal dignity.

113.play: ALENCON: Must he be then as shadow of himself?

114.play: Adorn his temples with a coronet,

115.play: And yet, in substance and authority,

116.play: Retain but privilege of a private man?

117.play: This proffer is absurd and reasonless.

118.play: CHARLES: 'Tis known already that I am possess'd

119.play: With more than half the Gallian territories,

120.play: And therein reverenced for their lawful king:

121.play: Shall I, for lucre of the rest unvanquish'd,

122.play: Detract so much from that prerogative,

123.play: As to be call'd but viceroy of the whole?

124.play: No, lord ambassador, I'll rather keep

125.play: That which I have than, coveting for more,

126.play: Be cast from possibility of all.

127.play: YORK: Insulting Charles! hast thou by secret means

128.play: Used intercession to obtain a league,

129.play: And, now the matter grows to compromise,

131.play: Either accept the title thou usurp'st,

132.play: Of benefit proceeding from our king

133.play: And not of any challenge of desert,

134.play: Or we will plague thee with incessant wars.

135.play: REIGNIER: My lord, you do not well in obstinacy

136.play: To cavil in the course of this contract:

137.play: If once it be neglected, ten to one

138.play: We shall not find like opportunity.

139.play: ALENCON: To say the truth, it is your policy

140.play: To save your subjects from such massacre

141.play: And ruthless slaughters as are daily seen

143.play: And therefore take this compact of a truce,

144.play: Although you break it when your pleasure serves.

145.play: WARWICK: How say'st thou, Charles? shall our condition stand?

147.play: Only reserved, you claim no interest

148.play: In any of our towns of garrison.

149.play: YORK: Then swear allegiance to his majesty,

150.play: As thou art knight, never to disobey

151.play: Nor be rebellious to the crown of England,

152.play: Thou, nor thy nobles, to the crown of England.

153.play: So, now dismiss your army when ye please:

154.play: Hang up your ensign, let your drums be still,

155.play: For here we entertain a solemn peace.

1.play: [Enter SUFFOLK in conference with KING HENRY VI, GLOUCESTER and EXETER]

2.play: KING HENRY VI: Your wondrous rare description, noble earl,

3.play: Of beauteous Margaret hath astonish'd me:

4.play: Her virtues graced with external gifts

5.play: Do breed love's settled passions in my heart:

6.play: And like as rigor of tempestuous gusts

7.play: Provokes the mightiest hulk against the tide,

8.play: So am I driven by breath of her renown

9.play: Either to suffer shipwreck or arrive

10.play: Where I may have fruition of her love.

11.play: SUFFOLK: Tush, my good lord, this superficial tale

12.play: Is but a preface of her worthy praise;

13.play: The chief perfections of that lovely dame

14.play: Had I sufficient skill to utter them,

15.play: Would make a volume of enticing lines,

16.play: Able to ravish any dull conceit:

17.play: And, which is more, she is not so divine,

18.play: So full-replete with choice of all delights,

19.play: But with as humble lowliness of mind

20.play: She is content to be at your command;

21.play: Command, I mean, of virtuous chaste intents,

22.play: To love and honour Henry as her lord.

23.play: KING HENRY VI: And otherwise will Henry ne'er presume.

24.play: Therefore, my lord protector, give consent

25.play: That Margaret may be England's royal queen.

26.play: GLOUCESTER: So should I give consent to flatter sin.

27.play: You know, my lord, your highness is betroth'd

28.play: Unto another lady of esteem:

29.play: How shall we then dispense with that contract,

30.play: And not deface your honour with reproach?

31.play: SUFFOLK: As doth a ruler with unlawful oaths;

32.play: Or one that, at a triumph having vow'd

33.play: To try his strength, forsaketh yet the lists

34.play: By reason of his adversary's odds:

35.play: A poor earl's daughter is unequal odds,

36.play: And therefore may be broke without offence.

37.play: GLOUCESTER: Why, what, I pray, is Margaret more than that?

38.play: Her father is no better than an earl,

39.play: Although in glorious titles he excel.

40.play: SUFFOLK: Yes, lord, her father is a king,

41.play: The King of Naples and Jerusalem;

42.play: And of such great authority in France

43.play: As his alliance will confirm our peace

44.play: And keep the Frenchmen in allegiance.

45.play: GLOUCESTER: And so the Earl of Armagnac may do,

46.play: Because he is near kinsman unto Charles.

47.play: EXETER: Beside, his wealth doth warrant a liberal dower,

48.play: Where Reignier sooner will receive than give.

49.play: SUFFOLK: A dower, my lords! disgrace not so your king,

50.play: That he should be so abject, base and poor,

51.play: To choose for wealth and not for perfect love.

52.play: Henry is able to enrich his queen

53.play: And not seek a queen to make him rich:

54.play: So worthless peasants bargain for their wives,

55.play: As market-men for oxen, sheep, or horse.

56.play: Marriage is a matter of more worth

57.play: Than to be dealt in by attorneyship;

58.play: Not whom we will, but whom his grace affects,

59.play: Must be companion of his nuptial bed:

60.play: And therefore, lords, since he affects her most,

61.play: It most of all these reasons bindeth us,

62.play: In our opinions she should be preferr'd.

63.play: For what is wedlock forced but a hell,

64.play: An age of discord and continual strife?

66.play: And is a pattern of celestial peace.

67.play: Whom should we match with Henry, being a king,

68.play: But Margaret, that is daughter to a king?

69.play: Her peerless feature, joined with her birth,

70.play: Approves her fit for none but for a king:

71.play: Her valiant courage and undaunted spirit,

72.play: More than in women commonly is seen,

73.play: Will answer our hope in issue of a king;

74.play: For Henry, son unto a conqueror,

75.play: Is likely to beget more conquerors,

76.play: If with a lady of so high resolve

77.play: As is fair Margaret he be link'd in love.

78.play: Then yield, my lords; and here conclude with me

79.play: That Margaret shall be queen, and none but she.

80.play: KING HENRY VI: Whether it be through force of your report,

81.play: My noble Lord of Suffolk, or for that

82.play: My tender youth was never yet attaint

83.play: With any passion of inflaming love,

84.play: I cannot tell; but this I am assured,

85.play: I feel such sharp dissension in my breast,

86.play: Such fierce alarums both of hope and fear,

87.play: As I am sick with working of my thoughts.

88.play: Take, therefore, shipping; post, my lord, to France;

89.play: Agree to any covenants, and procure

90.play: That Lady Margaret do vouchsafe to come

91.play: To cross the seas to England and be crown'd

92.play: King Henry's faithful and anointed queen:

93.play: For your expenses and sufficient charge,

94.play: Among the people gather up a tenth.

95.play: Be gone, I say; for, till you do return,

96.play: I rest perplexed with a thousand cares.

97.play: And you, good uncle, banish all offence:

98.play: If you do censure me by what you were,

99.play: Not what you are, I know it will excuse

100.play: This sudden execution of my will.

101.play: And so, conduct me where, from company,

102.play: I may revolve and ruminate my grief.

103.play: GLOUCESTER: Ay, grief, I fear me, both at first and last.

104.play: SUFFOLK: Thus Suffolk hath prevail'd; and thus he goes,

105.play: As did the youthful Paris once to Greece,

106.play: With hope to find the like event in love,

107.play: But prosper better than the Trojan did.

108.play: Margaret shall now be queen, and rule the king;

109.play: But I will rule both her, the king and realm.
